{ "content": "
 FloCard Internal API Guide

 Base URL: https://api.flocard.internal/v2

 Authentication:
 All API requests require a Bearer token in the Authorization header.
 Example: Authorization: Bearer <your_token>
 Tokens can be generated from the Developer Portal at dev.flocard.internal.

 Common Endpoints:

 GET /expenses
 Returns a paginated list of expense records for the authenticated user.

dpoints:

 GET /expenses
 Returns a paginated list of expense records for the authenticated user.
 Query params: page (int), limit (int, max 100), status (pending|approved|rejected)

 POST /expenses
 Creates a new expense entry.
 Body: { "amount": 500, "currency": "INR", "category": "travel", "description": "Client meeting cab" }

 POST /expenses/{id}/approve
 Approves a pending expense. Requires manager role.

meeting cab" }

 Approves a pending expense. Requires manager role.

 GET /reports/summary
 Returns a spending summary grouped by category for the current month.

 Error Codes:
 400 - Bad Request: missing or invalid fields
 401 - Unauthorized: invalid or expired token
 403 - Forbidden: insufficient permissions
 404 - Not Found: resource does not exist
 500 - Internal Server Error: contact the platform team

{ "content": "
 Code Review Standard Operating Procedure

 Purpose:
 To ensure all code changes meet FloCard quality and security standards before merging.

 Rules:
 1. Every pull request must have at least 2 approvals before merging.
 2. No self-merges are allowed, even for urgent hotfixes.
 3. All CI checks (lint, tests, security scan) must pass before review can be approved.
 4. PRs should be kept small — ideally under 400 lines of change.
 5.

review can be approved.
 4. PRs should be kept small — ideally under 400 lines of change.
 5. Reviewers must respond within 1 business day.

 What reviewers must check:
 - Correctness: Does the code do what the PR description says?
 - Security: No hardcoded secrets, no SQL injection risks, no exposed PII.

the PR description says?
 - Security: No hardcoded secrets, no SQL injection risks, no exposed PII.
 - Tests: Are unit and integration tests added or updated?
 - Documentation: Are new functions and APIs documented?
 - Performance: No obvious N+1 queries or blocking operations on the main thread.

 How to raise a PR:
 1. Push your branch to origin.
 2. Open a PR on GitHub with a clear title and description.
 3. Tag relevant reviewers and link the Jira ticket.
 4.

itHub with a clear title and description.
 3. Tag relevant reviewers and link the Jira ticket.
 4. Address all review comments before re-requesting review.

 Hotfix process:
 For production incidents, one approval is sufficient but the second must follow within 24 hours.
 All hotfixes must be accompanied by a post-mortem ticket in Jira.

{ "content": "
 FloCard is a sustainability-focused digital business platform founded in India.
 The company provides digital business cards, sustainability solutions, and climate-focused business tools.
 FloCard helps businesses reduce paper waste while supporting sustainability and net-zero initiatives.
 The platform enables users to create secure digital business cards that can be shared through QR codes, links, and OTP-based access.

eate secure digital business cards that can be shared through QR codes, links, and OTP-based access.\n FloCard also offers solutions such as carbon offsetting, GHG calculators, SDG alignment, climate finance support, and sustainability dashboards.\n The company promotes the #BetterPlanetTogether initiative to encourage organizations and individuals to contribute toward global sustainability goals.

tiative to encourage organizations and individuals to contribute toward global sustainability goals.
 FloCard supports businesses in aligning with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and climate action programs.
 The platform focuses on combining networking, environmental responsibility, and digital transformation into a unified ecosystem.
 FloCard operates through business, community, and sustainability-driven initiatives across multiple sectors.

operates through business, community, and sustainability-driven initiatives across multiple sectors.\n ", "metadata": { "title": "Company Overview", "tags": [ "company", "overview", "flocard", "sustainability", "digital-business-card" ], "type": "overview", "date": "2026-05-20" } }

{ "content": "
 Local Development Environment Setup

 Prerequisites:
 - Node.js v20 or higher
 - Python 3.11 or higher
 - Docker Desktop
 - Git configured with your FloCard email

 Step 1: Clone the repository
 git clone git@github.com:flocard/platform.git
 cd platform

 Step 2: Install backend dependencies
 cd server
 python -m venv venv
 venv/Scripts/activate (Windows) or source venv/bin/activate (Mac/Linux)
 pip install -r requirements.

nv/Scripts/activate (Windows) or source venv/bin/activate (Mac/Linux)
 pip install -r requirements.txt

 Step 3: Install frontend dependencies
 cd ../client
 npm install

 Step 4: Set up environment variables
 Copy .env.example to .env and fill in required values.
 Ask your team lead for the development API keys.

 Step 5: Start local services
 docker-compose up -d (starts PostgreSQL and Redis)

 Step 6: Run the backend
 cd server
 uvicorn api.

r-compose up -d (starts PostgreSQL and Redis)

 Step 6: Run the backend
 cd server
 uvicorn api.main:app --reload --port 8000

 Step 7: Run the frontend
 cd client
 npm run dev

 The app will be available at http://localhost:3000.
 API docs are at http://localhost:8000/docs.

{ "content": "
 New Employee Onboarding SOP

 This document describes the standard onboarding process for all new FloCard employees.

 Week 1 — Setup and Orientation
 Day 1: Collect your laptop from IT. Set up email, Slack, and VPN access.
 Day 2: Complete mandatory compliance and security training in the LMS portal.
 Day 3: Meet your buddy (assigned by HR) and attend the company orientation session.
 Day 4-5: Shadow your team and review relevant internal documentation.

company orientation session.
 Day 4-5: Shadow your team and review relevant internal documentation.

 Week 2 — Role Onboarding
 Engineering hires: Set up local dev environment using the Dev Setup Guide.
 Complete your first small task or bug fix with code review from your team lead.
 Non-engineering hires: Complete role-specific training modules in the LMS.

ew from your team lead.
 Non-engineering hires: Complete role-specific training modules in the LMS.

 Checklists:
 - IT access provisioned (email, Slack, GitHub, Jira)
 - HR documents signed (NDA, employment contract, POSH policy)
 - First 30-60-90 day goals set with manager
 - Added to team Slack channels and calendar invites

 Contacts:
 HR queries: ask@flocard.

{ "content": "
 Q: How do I request access to internal tools?
 A: Submit a request via the IT portal at ask@flocard.app. Approval takes 1-2 business days.

 Q: Who do I contact for payroll issues?
 A: Reach out to ask@flocard.app or raise a ticket in the HR portal under the Payroll section.

 Q: How do I join the engineering on-call rotation?
 A: Speak to your team lead. On-call rotations are managed in PagerDuty and shifts are weekly.

ion?
 A: Speak to your team lead. On-call rotations are managed in PagerDuty and shifts are weekly.

 Q: Where can I find the company org chart?
 A: The org chart is available on the internal Confluence page under Company > People > Org Chart.

 Q: What is the process for getting a new laptop?
 A: New hardware requests must be approved by your manager and submitted via the IT portal.

 Q: How do I reset my VPN credentials?
 A: Contact the IT helpdesk at ask@flocard.app.

DATA SCIENCE USING PYTHON Comprehensive Notes — All Units MCA — CBCS Curriculum — Ranchi University Theory: 60 Lectures | Tutorial: 15 Hours UNIT I — Introduction to Python 1.1 Keywords and Identifiers 1.1.1 Keywords Keywords are reserved words in Python that have special meaning to the interpreter. They cannot be used as identifiers (variable names, function names, class names, etc.). Python has 35 keywords (as of Python 3.

tifiers (variable names, function names, class names, etc.). Python has 35 keywords (as of Python 3.12): False, True, None — Boolean and null literals and, or, not — Logical operators if, elif, else — Conditional branching for, while — Looping constructs break, continue, pass — Loop control statements def, return, lambda — Function definitions class — Class definition try, except, finally, raise, with, as — Exception handling import, from, as — Module import global, nonlocal — Scope modifiers de

with, as — Exception handling import, from, as — Module import global, nonlocal — Scope modifiers del — Delete an object in, is — Membership and identity operators yield, async, await — Generator and coroutine keywords assert — Debugging assertion import keyword print(keyword.kwlist) # prints all keywords 1.1.2 Identifiers An identifier is a name given to entities like variables, functions, classes, modules, etc. Rules for Identifiers: Must start with a letter (a–z, A–Z) or underscore (_).

classes, modules, etc. Rules for Identifiers: Must start with a letter (a–z, A–Z) or underscore (_). Followed by letters, digits (0–9), or underscores. Cannot be a keyword. Case-sensitive: 'Name' and 'name' are different identifiers. No special characters (@, #, $, %, etc.) are allowed. No length limit, but convention suggests keeping names meaningful and concise. Naming Conventions (PEP 8): snake_case — used for variables and functions (e.g.

ningful and concise. Naming Conventions (PEP 8): snake_case — used for variables and functions (e.g., my_variable, calculate_area) CamelCase / PascalCase — used for class names (e.g., MyClass, StudentRecord) ALL_CAPS — used for constants (e.g., MAX_SIZE, PI) Leading underscore _name — convention for 'private' members Double leading underscore __name — name mangling in classes 1.2 Statements and Comments 1.2.1 Statements A statement is an instruction that Python can execute.

1.2 Statements and Comments 1.2.1 Statements A statement is an instruction that Python can execute. Simple Statement — occupies a single logical line. e.g., x = 5 Compound Statement — contains a group of other statements. e.g.

s a single logical line. e.g., x = 5 Compound Statement — contains a group of other statements. e.g., if, for, while, def, class blocks Multi-line Statements — use backslash \ for explicit line continuation, or implicit continuation inside [], {}, () Multiple Statements on one line — separated by semicolons: a = 1; b = 2 # Explicit line continuation total = 1 + 2 + \ 3 + 4 # Implicit continuation result = (10 + 20 + 30) 1.2.

licit line continuation total = 1 + 2 + \ 3 + 4 # Implicit continuation result = (10 + 20 + 30) 1.2.2 Comments Comments are non-executable text used to explain code to human readers. Single-line comment — starts with # symbol. e.g., # This is a comment Multi-line comment — multiple # lines or a triple-quoted string ('''/""") used as a docstring Docstrings — triple-quoted strings immediately after def/class — accessible via __doc__ attribute def greet(name): """This function greets the user.

fter def/class — accessible via __doc__ attribute def greet(name): """This function greets the user. Args: name (str): the user's name Returns: str """ return f"Hello, {name}!" 1.3 Variables A variable is a named memory location that holds a value. Python variables are dynamically typed — the type is determined at runtime. 1.3.

that holds a value. Python variables are dynamically typed — the type is determined at runtime. 1.3.1 Variable Assignment Simple Assignment — x = 10 Multiple Assignment — x = y = z = 0 Tuple Unpacking — a, b, c = 1, 2, 3 Augmented Assignment — x += 5 (equivalent to x = x + 5) Walrus Operator := — assigns inside an expression: if n := len(data) > 10: 1.3.2 Variable Types by Scope Local Variable — defined inside a function; accessible only within that function.

ble Types by Scope Local Variable — defined inside a function; accessible only within that function. Global Variable — defined outside all functions; accessible throughout the module. Free Variable — used in a nested function but defined in the enclosing function. Built-in Variable — predefined names in the builtins module (e.g., len, print, type). 1.4 Datatypes Everything in Python is an object with a type. Python is strongly and dynamically typed. 1.4.

atypes Everything in Python is an object with a type. Python is strongly and dynamically typed. 1.4.1 Numeric Types int — arbitrary precision integer. e.g., x = 42, x = -100, x = 0b1010 (binary), 0o77 (octal), 0xFF (hex) float — 64-bit double-precision floating-point. e.g., 3.14, -0.001, 2.5e10 complex — complex number with real and imaginary parts. e.g., z = 3 + 4j; z.real → 3.0; z.imag → 4.0 bool — subclass of int; only True (1) and False (0). 1.4.

g., z = 3 + 4j; z.real → 3.0; z.imag → 4.0 bool — subclass of int; only True (1) and False (0). 1.4.2 Sequence Types str — immutable sequence of Unicode characters. e.g., s = 'hello' list — mutable ordered sequence. e.g., lst = [1, 2, 3] tuple — immutable ordered sequence. e.g., t = (1, 2, 3) range — immutable arithmetic progression. e.g., range(0, 10, 2) bytes — immutable sequence of bytes. e.g., b'hello' bytearray — mutable version of bytes. 1.4.

0, 2) bytes — immutable sequence of bytes. e.g., b'hello' bytearray — mutable version of bytes. 1.4.3 Set Types set — mutable, unordered collection of unique elements. e.g., s = {1, 2, 3} frozenset — immutable version of set. Can be used as dict key. 1.4.4 Mapping Type dict — mutable mapping of key-value pairs. e.g., d = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25} 1.4.5 None Type NoneType — represents absence of value. Only one value: None. Often used as default return or null marker. 1.5 Type Conversion 1.5.

f value. Only one value: None. Often used as default return or null marker. 1.5 Type Conversion 1.5.1 Implicit (Automatic) Conversion Python automatically converts smaller types to larger types to prevent data loss during arithmetic. x = 3 + 4.5 # int + float → float: result is 7.5 y = True + 1 # bool + int → int: result is 2 1.5.2 Explicit (Type Casting) Conversion Programmer explicitly converts using built-in functions: int(x) — converts to integer. int('42') → 42; int(3.

r explicitly converts using built-in functions: int(x) — converts to integer. int('42') → 42; int(3.9) → 3 (truncates) float(x) — converts to float. float('3.14') → 3.14 str(x) — converts to string. str(100) → '100' bool(x) — converts to boolean. bool(0) → False; bool('') → False; everything else → True list(x) — converts iterable to list. list('abc') → ['a', 'b', 'c'] tuple(x) — converts iterable to tuple. set(x) — converts iterable to set (removes duplicates).

'c'] tuple(x) — converts iterable to tuple. set(x) — converts iterable to set (removes duplicates). dict(x) — creates dict from key-value pairs. dict(a=1, b=2) → {'a':1,'b':2} chr(n) / ord(c) — convert between character and ASCII/Unicode integer. hex(n) / oct(n) / bin(n) — integer to hex/octal/binary string. 1.6 I/O and Import 1.6.1 Input and Output input(prompt) — reads a string from standard input. Always returns a string. print(*objects, sep, end, file, flush) — writes output to stdout.

rd input. Always returns a string. print(*objects, sep, end, file, flush) — writes output to stdout. name = input('Enter your name: ') age = int(input('Enter age: ')) # must cast manually print(f'Hello {name}, you are {age} years old.') print('a', 'b', 'c', sep='-', end='\n') # a-b-c 1.6.2 Import import module — imports entire module. Access via module.

', sep='-', end='\n') # a-b-c 1.6.2 Import import module — imports entire module. Access via module.name from module import name — imports specific name into current namespace from module import * — imports all public names (discouraged) import module as alias — imports with an alias: import numpy as np __import__(name) — dynamic import at runtime 1.7 Operators 1.7.

ts with an alias: import numpy as np __import__(name) — dynamic import at runtime 1.7 Operators 1.7.1 Arithmetic Operators +, -, *, / — addition, subtraction, multiplication, true division (always float) // — floor division (integer result rounded towards negative infinity) % — modulus (remainder) ** — exponentiation. 2**10 → 1024 1.7.

lt rounded towards negative infinity) % — modulus (remainder) ** — exponentiation. 2**10 → 1024 1.7.2 Comparison Operators ==, != — equality and inequality <, >, <=, >= — relational comparisons is, is not — identity comparison (same object in memory) in, not in — membership test 1.7.3 Logical Operators and — returns first falsy value or last value if all truthy or — returns first truthy value or last value if all falsy not — inverts boolean value 1.7.

l truthy or — returns first truthy value or last value if all falsy not — inverts boolean value 1.7.4 Bitwise Operators &, |, ^ — bitwise AND, OR, XOR ~ — bitwise NOT (complement) <<, >> — left shift and right shift 1.7.5 Assignment Operators =, +=, -=, *=, /=, //=, %=, **=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>= — perform operation and assign result. 1.7.

rs =, +=, -=, *=, /=, //=, %=, **=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>= — perform operation and assign result. 1.7.6 Operator Precedence (High to Low) ** (exponentiation) +x, -x, ~x (unary) *, /, //, % +, - <<, >> &, ^, | Comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=, is, in) not and or 1.8 Namespace A namespace is a container that holds a mapping from names to objects. It prevents naming conflicts between different parts of a program. 1.8.

pping from names to objects. It prevents naming conflicts between different parts of a program. 1.8.1 Types of Namespaces Built-in Namespace — Contains built-in functions and exceptions like print, len, ValueError. Created at interpreter startup. Global Namespace — Module-level names. Created when module is imported/run and persists until interpreter exits. Local Namespace — Names inside a function. Created on function call; destroyed when function returns.

ocal Namespace — Names inside a function. Created on function call; destroyed when function returns. Enclosing Namespace — In nested functions, the namespace of the outer function. The LEGB Rule defines the order in which Python searches for a name: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in. x = 'global' def outer(): x = 'enclosing' def inner(): x = 'local' print(x) # 'local' inner() outer() UNIT II — Control Statements 2.1 Conditional Statements (if..else) 2.1.

x) # 'local' inner() outer() UNIT II — Control Statements 2.1 Conditional Statements (if..else) 2.1.1 if Statement Executes a block only if a condition is True. if condition: # body 2.1.2 if-else Statement if condition: # true block else: # false block 2.1.3 if-elif-else Ladder if condition1: # block1 elif condition2: # block2 elif condition3: # block3 else: # default block 2.1.4 Nested if An if statement inside another if block. Used for more complex condition checking. 2.1.

.4 Nested if An if statement inside another if block. Used for more complex condition checking. 2.1.5 Conditional Expression (Ternary Operator) value = expr_true if condition else expr_false # e.g., result = 'even' if n % 2 == 0 else 'odd' 2.2 Loops 2.2.1 for Loop Iterates over any iterable (list, string, range, dict, etc.). for item in iterable: # body for i in range(1, 11, 2): # 1,3,5,7,9 print(i) # Iterating with index for index, value in enumerate(['a','b','c']): print(index, value) 2.2.

int(i) # Iterating with index for index, value in enumerate(['a','b','c']): print(index, value) 2.2.2 while Loop Repeats body as long as condition remains True. Used when number of iterations is not known beforehand. while condition: # body # must modify condition to prevent infinite loop 2.2.3 Nested Loops A loop inside another loop. Outer loop controls rows; inner loop controls columns — commonly used for matrix operations and pattern printing. 2.

ols rows; inner loop controls columns — commonly used for matrix operations and pattern printing. 2.3 Loop Control Statements break — immediately exits the nearest enclosing loop, skipping the else clause if present. continue — skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one. pass — null statement — does nothing; used as a placeholder where syntax requires a statement.

one. pass — null statement — does nothing; used as a placeholder where syntax requires a statement. for i in range(10): if i == 3: continue # skip 3 if i == 7: break # stop at 7 print(i) # prints 0,1,2,4,5,6 # for-else / while-else for i in range(5): pass else: print('loop completed without break') 2.4 Functions A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task. It promotes modularity and DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle.

ode that performs a specific task. It promotes modularity and DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle. def function_name(parameters): '''docstring''' # body return value # optional 2.4.1 Return Statement A function without return implicitly returns None. Can return multiple values as a tuple: return x, y return exits the function immediately. 2.5 Function Arguments 2.5.1 Positional Arguments Matched by position. Order matters. 2.5.2 Keyword Arguments Matched by name. Order doesn't matter. e.g.

ched by position. Order matters. 2.5.2 Keyword Arguments Matched by name. Order doesn't matter. e.g., greet(name='Alice', age=30) 2.5.3 Default Arguments Parameter has a default value if no argument is passed. def greet(name, greeting='Hello'): return f'{greeting}, {name}' 2.5.4 Variable-length Arguments *args — collects extra positional arguments into a tuple. def func(*args): ... **kwargs — collects extra keyword arguments into a dict. def func(**kwargs): ...

ef func(*args): ... **kwargs — collects extra keyword arguments into a dict. def func(**kwargs): ... def info(*args, **kwargs): print(args) # tuple print(kwargs) # dict info(1, 2, name='Bob', city='NY') 2.5.5 Keyword-Only Arguments Arguments after * must be passed as keyword arguments. def func(a, b, *, c, d): # c, d must be keyword args pass 2.5.6 Positional-Only Arguments (Python 3.8+) Arguments before / must be passed positionally. def func(a, b, /, c, d): # a, b must be positional pass 2.

nts before / must be passed positionally. def func(a, b, /, c, d): # a, b must be positional pass 2.6 Recursion A function that calls itself. Requires a base case (termination condition) to prevent infinite recursion. def factorial(n): if n == 0: return 1 # base case return n * factorial(n - 1) # recursive case def fibonacci(n): if n <= 1: return n return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2) Stack Overflow (RecursionError) — Python default recursion limit is 1000. Use sys.

+ fibonacci(n-2) Stack Overflow (RecursionError) — Python default recursion limit is 1000. Use sys.setrecursionlimit(n) to change. Tail Recursion — Python doesn't optimize tail calls; iterative approach is usually preferred. Memoization — Cache results of recursive calls using functools.lru_cache to avoid repeated computation. 2.7 Anonymous Functions (Lambda) A lambda is a small anonymous function defined with the lambda keyword. Can have any number of arguments but only one expression.

function defined with the lambda keyword. Can have any number of arguments but only one expression. lambda arguments: expression square = lambda x: x**2 add = lambda x, y: x + y # Common use with map, filter, sorted nums = [5, 2, 8, 1] nums.sort(key=lambda x: -x) # sort descending map(func, iterable) — applies func to each element, returns map object. filter(func, iterable) — keeps elements where func returns True. sorted(iterable, key=func) — sorts using key function.

le) — keeps elements where func returns True. sorted(iterable, key=func) — sorts using key function. reduce(func, iterable) — from functools; applies func cumulatively. 2.8 Global, Local, and Nonlocal global — inside a function, declares that a variable refers to the module-level global, not a new local. nonlocal — inside a nested function, declares that a variable refers to the enclosing function's scope, not global or local.

ed function, declares that a variable refers to the enclosing function's scope, not global or local. x = 10 def modify(): global x x = 20 # modifies global x def outer(): y = 10 def inner(): nonlocal y y = 20 # modifies outer's y inner() 2.9 Modules and Packages 2.9.1 Modules A module is a .py file containing Python code (functions, classes, variables). It provides code reusability and organization.

ntaining Python code (functions, classes, variables). It provides code reusability and organization. __name__ == '__main__' — Code inside this block runs only when file is executed directly, not when imported. dir(module) — lists all names defined in a module. __init__.py — makes a directory a package (can be empty in Python 3). 2.9.2 Packages A package is a directory containing an __init__.py file and one or more modules or sub-packages. It provides hierarchical module organization.

nit__.py file and one or more modules or sub-packages. It provides hierarchical module organization. # Structure mypackage/ __init__.py module1.py subpackage/ __init__.py module2.py # Importing from mypackage.module1 import my_function from mypackage.subpackage import module2 UNIT III — Datatypes in Depth 3.1 Numbers Covered in Unit I. Key additions: Python's int has unlimited precision. Floating-point follows IEEE 754. Useful modules: math, cmath, decimal (for precise decimals), fractions.

ting-point follows IEEE 754. Useful modules: math, cmath, decimal (for precise decimals), fractions. import math math.sqrt(16) # 4.0 math.ceil(3.1) # 4 math.floor(3.9) # 3 math.pi, math.e 3.2 List An ordered, mutable, heterogeneous collection. Allows duplicate values. Index starts at 0. 3.2.1 Creating Lists lst = [1, 2, 3] lst = list(range(10)) lst = [x**2 for x in range(5)] # list comprehension 3.2.

Lists lst = [1, 2, 3] lst = list(range(10)) lst = [x**2 for x in range(5)] # list comprehension 3.2.2 List Methods append(x) — adds x to end — O(1) insert(i, x) — inserts x at index i — O(n) extend(iterable) — appends all items of iterable — O(k) remove(x) — removes first occurrence of x — O(n) pop(i) — removes and returns item at index i (default: -1) — O(n) index(x) — returns index of first occurrence — O(n) count(x) — counts occurrences of x sort(key, reverse) — in-place sort — O(n log n) rev

rrence — O(n) count(x) — counts occurrences of x sort(key, reverse) — in-place sort — O(n log n) reverse() — reverses in place copy() — shallow copy of list clear() — removes all elements 3.2.3 Slicing lst[start:stop:step] lst[1:4] # elements 1,2,3 lst[::-1] # reversed copy lst[::2] # every other element 3.2.4 List Comprehension [expression for item in iterable if condition] even_squares = [x**2 for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0] 3.3 Tuple An ordered, immutable sequence. Faster than lists.
[x**2 for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0] 3.3 Tuple An ordered, immutable sequence. Faster than lists. Hashable (if elements are hashable), so can be used as dict keys or set elements. 3.3.

r than lists. op uses this protocol internally. class Counter: def __init__(self, limit): self.limit = limit self.current = 0 def __iter__(self): return self def __next__(self): if self.current >= self.limit: raise StopIteration self.current += 1 return self.current 3.12.2 Generator A generator function uses yield to produce values lazily (one at a time). on uses yield to produce values lazily (one at a time). unction that captures variables from the enclosing scope even after the outer function has returned. def make_multiplier(n): def multiply(x): return x * n # n is captured from enclosing scope return multiply double = make_multiplier(2) print(double(5)) # 10 3.12.4 Decorators A decorator is a callable that wraps another function, modifying or extending its behavior without changing its source code. def timer(func): import time def wrapper(*args, **kwargs): start = time.

Fast but may not produce real words. Lemmatization — uses vocabulary and morphological analysis to return the base (dictionary) form of a word. e.g., 'better' → 'good'.

and morphological analysis to return the base (dictionary) form of a word. e.g., 'better' → 'good'. Slower but more accurate. from nltk.stem import PorterStemmer, SnowballStemmer from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer ps = PorterStemmer() ps.stem('running') # 'run' ps.stem('studies') # 'studi' lemma = WordNetLemmatizer() lemma.lemmatize('better', pos='a') # 'good' lemma.lemmatize('running', pos='v') # 'run' UNIT V — Visualizing Data 5.

C / BIC — information criteria for model comparison — lower is better UNIT VII — Gradient Descent 7.1 Idea Behind Gradient Descent Gradient Descent is an iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize a cost/loss function by moving in the direction of steepest descent (negative gradient). It is the backbone of training machine learning models. Intuition: Imagine being blindfolded on a hilly terrain. You take small steps downhill until you reach the lowest point (minimum).

folded on a hilly terrain. You take small steps downhill until you reach the lowest point (minimum). Cost Function J(θ) — measures model error — e.g., MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification Gradient ∇J(θ) — vector of partial derivatives of J with respect to each parameter θ. Points in direction of steepest increase. Update Rule — θ = θ - α × ∇J(θ). We subtract gradient to go downhill. 7.

ction of steepest increase. Update Rule — θ = θ - α × ∇J(θ). We subtract gradient to go downhill. 7.2 Estimating the Gradient Analytical Gradient — compute derivative mathematically — exact but only possible for simple functions. Numerical Gradient (Finite Difference) — [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h for small h — used to check analytical gradients. Automatic Differentiation (AutoDiff) — used by PyTorch and TensorFlow — computes exact gradients efficiently using computation graphs and chain rule.

Torch and TensorFlow — computes exact gradients efficiently using computation graphs and chain rule. # Simple gradient descent for y = (x-3)^2 def f(x): return (x-3)**2 def grad_f(x): return 2*(x-3) # df/dx x = 10.0 # initial guess learning_rate = 0.1 for i in range(50): x = x - learning_rate * grad_f(x) print(f'Minimum at x = {x:.4f}') # ≈ 3.0 7.3 Using the Gradient Descent 7.3.1 Vanilla (Batch) Gradient Descent Uses ALL training samples to compute gradient.

adient Descent 7.3.1 Vanilla (Batch) Gradient Descent Uses ALL training samples to compute gradient. Stable but very slow for large datasets. One update per epoch. 7.3.2 Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Uses ONE random training sample per update. Noisy and erratic but fast — can escape local minima. Many updates per epoch. 7.3.3 Mini-Batch Gradient Descent Uses a small batch (32, 64, 128 samples). Best of both worlds — efficient and relatively stable. Used in deep learning.

(32, 64, 128 samples). Best of both worlds — efficient and relatively stable. atch_size] gradient = compute_gradient(X_batch, y_batch, theta) theta -= learning_rate * gradient 7.4 Choosing the Right Step Size (Learning Rate α) Too small α — very slow convergence — takes many epochs to reach minimum. Too large α — overshooting — diverges, cost may increase or oscillate. Just right α — converges smoothly to minimum. 7.4.

oting — diverges, cost may increase or oscillate. Just right α — converges smoothly to minimum. 7.4.1 Learning Rate Schedules Constant LR — fixed value throughout training Step Decay — reduce LR by factor every k epochs Exponential Decay — α = α0 × e^(-kt) Cosine Annealing — smoothly reduces LR following cosine curve ReduceLROnPlateau — automatically reduces when validation loss stops improving (Keras callback) 7.4.

ReduceLROnPlateau — automatically reduces when validation loss stops improving (Keras callback) 7.4.2 Adaptive Optimizers Momentum — accumulates velocity in directions of persistent gradient — accelerates convergence AdaGrad — adapts LR per-parameter — smaller updates for frequent features RMSprop — fixes AdaGrad's diminishing LR problem using moving average of squared gradients Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) — combines Momentum + RMSprop. Most popular optimizer in deep learning.

(Adaptive Moment Estimation) — combines Momentum + RMSprop. Most popular optimizer in deep learning. AdamW — Adam with weight decay — better generalization # Linear regression with gradient descent (from scratch) import numpy as np def gradient_descent_lr(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=1000): m, n = X.shape theta = np.zeros(n) losses = [] for epoch in range(epochs): predictions = X @ theta errors = predictions - y gradient = (2/m) * X.T @ errors theta -= lr * gradient loss = np.mean(errors**2) losses.
ictions - y gradient = (2/m) * X.T @ errors theta -= lr * gradient loss = np.mean(errors**2) losses.append(loss) return theta, losses 7.

errors theta -= lr * gradient loss = np.mean(errors**2) losses.append(loss) return theta, losses 7.5 Stochastic Gradient Descent — Deep Dive Epoch — one complete pass through the entire training dataset Iteration — one parameter update (one batch processed) Convergence — when loss stops decreasing significantly — monitor training and validation loss Overfitting — training loss low but validation loss high — add regularization, dropout, more data Underfitting — both losses high — more complex mo

loss high — add regularization, dropout, more data Underfitting — both losses high — more complex model or more training Early Stopping — stop training when validation loss starts increasing Note: In SGD, each update uses a different sample, making the path to minimum noisy. This noise can actually help escape shallow local minima, making SGD useful for non-convex optimization in neural networks. UNIT VIII — Data 8.1 Stdin and Stdout sys.

ng and selling of goods, services, and information over electronic networks, primarily the Internet. geographical barriers, reducing transaction costs, and enabling round-the-clock business operations. funds are transferred from the buyer's bank to the seller's bank account after a transaction. 3.11.1 Settlement Process Steps Step 1 — Authorisation: payment gateway sends card details to the acquiring bank; acquiring bank forwards to card network (Visa/Mastercard); card network contacts issuing bank; issuing bank approves or declines. Step 2 — Capture: merchant captures the approved transaction — funds are reserved in customer's account.

2 — Capture: merchant captures the approved transaction — funds are reserved in customer's account. Step 3 — Batching: merchant groups captured transactions into a settlement batch (usually end of day). Step 4 — Clearing: card network processes the batch; calculates interchange fees; routes to appropriate banks. Step 5 — Settlement: acquiring bank deposits funds into merchant account, minus processing fees. Typically T+1 to T+3 days.

quiring bank deposits funds into merchant account, minus processing fees. Typically T+1 to T+3 days. Interchange Fee: fee paid by acquiring bank to issuing bank for each transaction — typically 1.5–2.5% of transaction value. Merchant Discount Rate (MDR): total fee charged to merchant for accepting card payments — includes interchange + processor margin. Chargebacks: disputed transactions where funds are reversed from merchant to customer — merchants must provide evidence to contest.

ons where funds are reversed from merchant to customer — merchants must provide evidence to contest. Markets can be physical (traditional bazaars, stock exchanges) or virtual (electronic markets).

ion. Markets can be physical (traditional bazaars, stock exchanges) or virtual (electronic markets). Traditional markets have geographical and temporal limitations — requiring physical presence and operating during specific hours. E-commerce has fundamentally transformed market structures. 5.

hange information about prices, products, and services, and to complete transactions electronically. UNIT VI — Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) 6.1 Definition of EDI Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) is the computer-to-computer exchange of structured business documents between organisations in a standardised electronic format.

exchange of structured business documents between organisations in a standardised electronic format. EDI replaces paper-based documents such as purchase orders, invoices, shipping notices, and payment confirmations with standardised electronic messages exchanged directly between business information systems. EDI eliminates manual data entry, reduces errors, accelerates transaction processing, and enables automated business workflows between trading partners.

celerates transaction processing, and enables automated business workflows between trading partners. Structured Format: EDI uses strict, standardised document formats — both parties must use the same standard. Computer-to-Computer: EDI transactions are processed automatically by software without human intervention. es inter-organisational communication — between businesses, governments, and their trading partners. NETWORK SECURITY AND CRYPTOGRAPHY Comprehensive Study Notes — All 8 Units MCA | CBCS Curriculum | Ranchi University Theory: 60 Lectures | Tutorial: 15 Hours PART A — BASICS OF NETWORKING UNIT I — Fundamental Concepts: Communications Model, Protocol and Architecture 1.1 Communications Model A Communications Model describes the fundamental mechanism for the exchange of data between two parties.

mmunications Model describes the fundamental mechanism for the exchange of data between two parties. The simplest model involves a source, transmission system, and destination, but in practice many intermediate components and processes are involved. 1.1.1 Key Components of a Communication System Source (Transmitter): generates data to be transmitted — a computer, telephone, camera, or sensor. Transmitter: converts data into a transmittable signal form (electrical, optical, radio waves).

sor. Transmitter: converts data into a transmittable signal form (electrical, optical, radio waves). Performs encoding, modulation, and amplification. Transmission System: the physical medium or channel carrying the signal — wire, fiber optic cable, radio waves, satellite. Receiver: accepts the incoming signal and converts it back into the original data form. Performs demodulation and decoding. Destination: the ultimate consumer of the transmitted data — computer, speaker, display, or person. 1.

stination: the ultimate consumer of the transmitted data — computer, speaker, display, or person. 1.1.2 Key Tasks in Communication Signal Generation: creating the physical signal to carry information. Synchronisation: ensuring sender and receiver agree on timing to correctly interpret signal boundaries. Interface Management: managing the interface between the device and the communication medium.

aries. Interface Management: managing the interface between the device and the communication medium. Signal Propagation Management: handling signal degradation (attenuation), noise, and interference over distance. Addressing and Routing: identifying source and destination uniquely; finding the path through the network. Error Detection and Correction: detecting and recovering from transmission errors using checksums, CRC, ARQ.

tection and Correction: detecting and recovering from transmission errors using checksums, CRC, ARQ. Flow Control: preventing a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. Security: ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of transmitted data. 1.2 Basic Protocol Architecture A protocol is a set of rules governing communication between entities at the same level (same layer) in different systems.

rules governing communication between entities at the same level (same layer) in different systems. A protocol architecture organises these rules into a structured hierarchy of layers, where each layer provides services to the layer above it and uses services from the layer below. 1.2.1 Why Layering? Breaks a complex communication problem into manageable sub-problems. Each layer can be designed, implemented, and tested independently.

lem into manageable sub-problems. Each layer can be designed, implemented, and tested independently. Changes in one layer do not affect others (encapsulation principle). Enables interoperability between different vendors and technologies. 1.2.2 Protocol Data Units (PDUs) Data: raw user data passed from application layer. Segment: transport-layer PDU (TCP) — includes port numbers and sequence information. Packet: network-layer PDU — includes source and destination IP addresses.

and sequence information. Packet: network-layer PDU — includes source and destination IP addresses. Frame: data-link-layer PDU — includes MAC addresses and error-checking fields. Bits: physical-layer PDU — actual electrical/optical/radio signals. 1.3 The OSI Reference Model The Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model, developed by ISO in 1984, defines a seven-layer conceptual framework for network communication. It standardises how data flows from application to physical medium and back. 1.3.

ork communication. It standardises how data flows from application to physical medium and back. 1.3.1 OSI Layers — Top to Bottom Layer 7 — Application Layer Provides network services directly to end-user applications. Handles user authentication, privacy, and identifies communication partners. Protocols: HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, DHCP, SNMP, Telnet, SSH. Functions: file transfers, email, remote login, network management, directory services. PDU: Data (Message).

s: file transfers, email, remote login, network management, directory services. PDU: Data (Message). Layer 6 — Presentation Layer Translates data between the application layer format and the network format. Handles data syntax, semantics, and transformation. Functions: data encoding/decoding (ASCII, EBCDIC, Unicode), compression (JPEG, MPEG), encryption/decryption (SSL/TLS). PDU: Data. Layer 5 — Session Layer Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions (dialogues) between applications.

er 5 — Session Layer Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions (dialogues) between applications. Functions: session establishment, maintenance, and termination. Synchronisation checkpoints for recovery. Dialog control (half-duplex vs full-duplex). Protocols: NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP. Layer 4 — Transport Layer Provides end-to-end, reliable data transfer between processes on different hosts.

— Transport Layer Provides end-to-end, reliable data transfer between processes on different hosts. Functions: segmentation and reassembly of data, port addressing, connection control (connection-oriented vs connectionless), flow control, error recovery. Protocols: TCP (reliable, connection-oriented), UDP (unreliable, connectionless). PDU: Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP).

liable, connection-oriented), UDP (unreliable, connectionless). PDU: Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP). Layer 3 — Network Layer Handles logical addressing and routing — moving packets from source to destination across multiple networks. Functions: logical addressing (IP addresses), routing, packet forwarding, fragmentation and reassembly. Protocols: IP (IPv4, IPv6), ICMP, IGMP, ARP, OSPF, BGP, RIP. Devices: Routers, Layer-3 switches. PDU: Packet.

: IP (IPv4, IPv6), ICMP, IGMP, ARP, OSPF, BGP, RIP. Devices: Routers, Layer-3 switches. PDU: Packet. Layer 2 — Data Link Layer Provides reliable transmission of data frames between directly connected nodes. Handles physical addressing and error detection. Functions: framing, physical (MAC) addressing, error detection (CRC), flow control, media access control. Sub-layers: LLC (Logical Link Control — flow/error control) and MAC (Media Access Control — addressing/media access).

Logical Link Control — flow/error control) and MAC (Media Access Control — addressing/media access). Protocols: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay. Devices: Switches, Bridges, Network Interface Cards (NICs). PDU: Frame. Layer 1 — Physical Layer Transmits raw bits over a physical medium. Defines electrical, mechanical, and procedural specifications. Functions: bit transmission, signal encoding, data rate specification, physical topology, synchronisation.

ons: bit transmission, signal encoding, data rate specification, physical topology, synchronisation. Standards: RS-232, USB, Ethernet physical standards, DSL, SONET, Bluetooth physical. Devices: Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Connectors, Modems. PDU: Bits (Signals). Key Point: OSI is a reference model — most real implementations use TCP/IP. OSI layers 5, 6, and 7 are often merged into the TCP/IP Application layer. 1.

mentations use TCP/IP. OSI layers 5, 6, and 7 are often merged into the TCP/IP Application layer. 1.4 The TCP/IP Protocol Architecture The TCP/IP model is the practical, de-facto standard for internet communications. It has 4 layers (sometimes shown as 5 when the Physical layer is separated from Data Link). 1.4.1 TCP/IP Layers Application Layer (Layers 5-7 of OSI): supports network applications directly. Protocols: HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, SSH, SNMP, Telnet.

of OSI): supports network applications directly. Protocols: HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, SSH, SNMP, Telnet. Transport Layer (Layer 4 of OSI): end-to-end communication. Protocols: TCP (reliable, ordered, error-checked) and UDP (fast, connectionless). Internet Layer (Layer 3 of OSI): addressing and routing. Protocols: IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, ARP. Network Access Layer (Layers 1-2 of OSI): physical transmission and data-link framing. Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP. 1.4.

s Layer (Layers 1-2 of OSI): physical transmission and data-link framing. Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP. 1.4.2 TCP — Transmission Control Protocol Connection-oriented: three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) before data transfer. Reliable: guarantees all packets arrive, in order, with no duplication. Flow Control: sliding window mechanism prevents receiver overflow. Congestion Control: algorithms (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit) prevent network congestion.

Control: algorithms (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit) prevent network congestion. Full-Duplex: simultaneous bidirectional data transfer. Use Cases: HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, SSH — any application requiring reliable delivery. 1.4.3 UDP — User Datagram Protocol Connectionless: no handshake — sends datagrams immediately. Unreliable: no guarantee of delivery, ordering, or duplicate protection. Low Overhead: minimal header (8 bytes vs TCP's 20+ bytes) — very fast.

ing, or duplicate protection. Low Overhead: minimal header (8 bytes vs TCP's 20+ bytes) — very fast. Use Cases: DNS, DHCP, VoIP, video streaming, online gaming, IoT — where speed matters more than reliability. 1.4.4 IPv4 vs IPv6 IPv4: 32-bit addresses (4 octets) — approximately 4.3 billion addresses. Written as dotted decimal: 192.168.1.1. Running out of available addresses. IPv6: 128-bit addresses — 340 undecillion addresses. Written in hexadecimal groups: 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334.

UNIT II — Communication Techniques 2.1 Transmission of Data Data transmission is the process of sending digital or analog data over a communication channel. The choice of transmission technique depends on the medium, distance, required speed, and cost. 2.

. The choice of transmission technique depends on the medium, distance, required speed, and cost. 2.2 Analog and Digital Data 2.2.1 Analog Data Analog data has continuously varying values — an infinite number of possible values within any range. Examples: human voice (continuous sound waves), temperature readings, vinyl audio. Analog Signal: continuous waveform. Characterised by amplitude (height), frequency (cycles/sec = Hz), and phase (position in cycle).

rm. Characterised by amplitude (height), frequency (cycles/sec = Hz), and phase (position in cycle). Bandwidth: range of frequencies in the signal — determines information-carrying capacity. Noise Susceptibility: analog signals degrade with distance and noise — amplifiers also amplify noise. 2.2.2 Digital Data Digital data has discrete values — typically binary (0s and 1s). Examples: computer files, digital audio (CDs, MP3), digital images (JPEG, PNG).

binary (0s and 1s). Examples: computer files, digital audio (CDs, MP3), digital images (JPEG, PNG). Digital Signal: discrete voltage levels representing binary 0 and 1. Pulses with defined timing. Advantages: immune to noise (can regenerate clean signals), easy encryption, easy to process and store. Nyquist Theorem: to accurately sample an analog signal, the sampling rate must be at least twice the highest frequency in the signal (Nyquist rate).

Up to 10–20Gbps. mmWave and sub-6GHz bands. 2.5 Asynchronous Transmission In asynchronous transmission, data is sent one character (byte) at a time with no common clock between sender and receiver.

ssion, data is sent one character (byte) at a time with no common clock between sender and receiver. Each character is framed by a start bit and one or two stop bits. Start Bit: signals the beginning of a character frame — transitions line from idle (high) to low (0). Data Bits: 5–8 bits carrying the actual character data. Parity Bit (optional): adds a bit for basic error detection — even parity or odd parity.

acter data. Parity Bit (optional): adds a bit for basic error detection — even parity or odd parity. Stop Bit(s): 1 or 2 bits marking the end of the character, returning line to idle state. Advantages: simple, inexpensive, tolerant of timing variations between devices. Disadvantages: overhead (start/stop bits = 20–30% of total bits) reduces efficiency. Suitable only for low-speed devices. Examples: RS-232 serial ports, keyboard/mouse interfaces, old modems, IoT sensors. 2.

speed devices. Examples: RS-232 serial ports, keyboard/mouse interfaces, old modems, IoT sensors. 2.6 Synchronous Transmission In synchronous transmission, data is sent as a continuous stream of bits with the sender and receiver sharing a common clock signal. Data is grouped into blocks (frames) rather than individual characters. Clock Synchronisation: sender embeds clock signal in data stream (e.g., Manchester encoding) or sends a separate clock signal. Receiver locks its clock to sender's.

(e.g., Manchester encoding) or sends a separate clock signal. Receiver locks its clock to sender's. Framing: blocks of data delineated by special flags or preamble patterns (e.g., 0x7E in HDLC, 10101010 preamble in Ethernet). Advantages: much higher efficiency — no per-character overhead. Better suited for high-speed data transfer. Disadvantages: more complex, requires clock synchronisation, entire block must be retransmitted on error.

vantages: more complex, requires clock synchronisation, entire block must be retransmitted on error. Examples: Ethernet, SONET/SDH, USB, HDLC, T1/T3 lines, satellite communications. UNIT III — Multiplexing and Switching Networks 3.1 Multiplexing Multiplexing is the process of combining multiple signals from multiple sources into a single composite signal for transmission over a shared medium, and then separating them at the receiving end.

mposite signal for transmission over a shared medium, and then separating them at the receiving end. It allows efficient use of expensive high-capacity transmission links. Multiplexer (MUX): device at the sender side that combines multiple signals into one. Demultiplexer (DEMUX): device at the receiver side that separates the combined signal back into individual signals. Purpose: share expensive transmission media (fiber, satellite bandwidth) among multiple users or channels. 3.

share expensive transmission media (fiber, satellite bandwidth) among multiple users or channels. 3.2 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) FDM divides the total available bandwidth of a channel into multiple non-overlapping frequency bands (sub-channels), each carrying a separate signal simultaneously. Used in analog systems. Principle: each signal is modulated to a different carrier frequency. Guard bands prevent interference between adjacent channels.

ulated to a different carrier frequency. Guard bands prevent interference between adjacent channels. Guard Bands: unused frequency gaps between channels to prevent overlap and interference. Modulation: each channel is modulated (AM, FM, or SSB) to its assigned frequency band. Advantages: simple, all channels transmitted simultaneously, no synchronisation needed. Disadvantages: bandwidth wastage in guard bands, susceptible to intermodulation noise, inflexible channel allocation.

ndwidth wastage in guard bands, susceptible to intermodulation noise, inflexible channel allocation. Applications: AM/FM radio broadcasting, cable TV, traditional telephone (PSTN), DSL (ADSL), CATV. 3.2.1 OFDM — Orthogonal FDM An advanced form of FDM where sub-channels are mathematically orthogonal (overlapping but non-interfering). Used in Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n/ac), 4G LTE, 5G, DSL (VDSL2), and digital broadcast. 3.

non-interfering). Used in Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n/ac), 4G LTE, 5G, DSL (VDSL2), and digital broadcast. 3.3 Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing (STDM) TDM divides the transmission time into fixed time slots. Each signal is assigned a specific, recurring time slot in a repeating frame. All slots are fixed regardless of whether the source has data to send. Frame Structure: time divided into N equal slots per frame. Slot 1 = Channel 1, Slot 2 = Channel 2, etc. — always.

e: time divided into N equal slots per frame. Slot 1 = Channel 1, Slot 2 = Channel 2, etc. — always. Synchronisation: receiver and sender share a common clock; receiver knows exactly which channel occupies each slot. Advantages: simple, deterministic timing, guaranteed bandwidth per channel, no addressing needed. Disadvantages: wasteful — if a channel has no data, its slot is empty (wasted bandwidth). Inflexible allocation.

s: wasteful — if a channel has no data, its slot is empty (wasted bandwidth). Inflexible allocation. Applications: T1/E1 telephone lines (24/30 channels × 64kbps), SONET/SDH, ISDN, PDH. T1 Line: 24 channels × 64kbps (8 bits × 8000 samples/sec) = 1.544 Mbps total data rate. 3.4 Statistical Time Division Multiplexing (STDM / StatMux) Statistical TDM (also called Asynchronous TDM) dynamically allocates time slots only to channels that have data to send.

o called Asynchronous TDM) dynamically allocates time slots only to channels that have data to send. Time slots are not pre-assigned — they are allocated on demand. Dynamic Allocation: MUX buffers data from sources and transmits only when data is available. Unused capacity redistributed to active channels. Addressing Overhead: each slot must include a channel identifier (address) since slots are not pre-assigned. Advantages: much higher efficiency than synchronous TDM — no wasted slots.

ots are not pre-assigned. Advantages: much higher efficiency than synchronous TDM — no wasted slots. Can support more channels than physical bandwidth would allow (statistical multiplexing gain). Disadvantages: variable delays (jitter), more complex hardware, addressing overhead, potential buffer overflow. Applications: packet-switched networks (Internet), ATM, Frame Relay, DSL modems, broadband Internet. 3.

lications: packet-switched networks (Internet), ATM, Frame Relay, DSL modems, broadband Internet. 3.5 Switching Networks In a switched network, a data path is established between source and destination. Unlike broadcast networks (shared bus), switching establishes point-to-point connections through intermediate switching nodes (switches/routers). Switching Node: intermediate device (switch, router) that receives data on an incoming link and forwards it on the appropriate outgoing link.

ch, router) that receives data on an incoming link and forwards it on the appropriate outgoing link. Switching Fabric: internal mechanism within a switch for moving data from input to output ports. 3.6 Circuit-Switching Networks In circuit switching, a dedicated communication path (circuit) is established between source and destination BEFORE data transmission begins. Resources are reserved for the entire duration of the call. 3.6.

on BEFORE data transmission begins. Resources are reserved for the entire duration of the call. 3.6.1 Three Phases of Circuit Switching Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment: signalling messages travel from source to destination, reserving resources (bandwidth, buffers) along the path. A dedicated circuit is established. Phase 2 — Data Transfer: data flows through the established circuit at a guaranteed, constant rate with minimal delay.

nsfer: data flows through the established circuit at a guaranteed, constant rate with minimal delay. Phase 3 — Circuit Disconnection: either party initiates teardown; signalling messages release reserved resources along the path. 3.6.2 Circuit-Switching Concepts Space Division Switching: physically separate paths created for each circuit. Crossbar switch matrix — electromagnetic contacts connect input to output lines.

d for each circuit. Crossbar switch matrix — electromagnetic contacts connect input to output lines. Time Division Switching (TDS): uses TDM principles — circuit assigned to a specific time slot. More efficient than space division. Used in modern digital telephone switches (PBX, CO switches). FDM-based Circuit Switching: each circuit assigned a specific frequency band (used in older analog telephone systems).

Switching: each circuit assigned a specific frequency band (used in older analog telephone systems). Blocking: if all circuits between two nodes are occupied, new connection requests are blocked (Grade of Service metric). Advantages: guaranteed QoS, constant delay, no overhead per data unit once circuit is established, simple data transfer. Disadvantages: wasteful during idle periods (bandwidth reserved but unused), setup delay, inflexible, poor for bursty data traffic.

idle periods (bandwidth reserved but unused), setup delay, inflexible, poor for bursty data traffic. Examples: traditional PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network), ISDN. 3.7 Packet-Switching Principles Packet switching divides data into packets that are independently routed through the network. No dedicated circuit — resources are shared dynamically. 3.7.1 Datagram Packet Switching Connectionless: each packet independently routed. Different packets of the same message may take different paths.

s: each packet independently routed. Different packets of the same message may take different paths. Routing: each node makes independent routing decision based on destination address in packet header. Reordering: packets may arrive out of order — receiver must reassemble. Robustness: if a node fails, packets are rerouted automatically. Examples: IP (the Internet Protocol) — the foundation of the Internet. 3.7.

rerouted automatically. Examples: IP (the Internet Protocol) — the foundation of the Internet. 3.7.2 Virtual Circuit Packet Switching Connection-oriented: a virtual circuit path is established before transmission. All packets follow the same path. VCI (Virtual Circuit Identifier): a short label in each packet's header (not full destination address) used by switches to forward packets. In-Order Delivery: since all packets follow the same path, they arrive in order.

to forward packets. In-Order Delivery: since all packets follow the same path, they arrive in order. Phases: setup → data transfer → teardown (similar to circuit switching but no resource reservation). Examples: ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode), Frame Relay, MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching). 3.7.3 Comparison: Circuit Switching vs Packet Switching Dedicated Resources: Circuit — yes, guaranteed. Packet — no, shared dynamically. Delay: Circuit — constant, predictable.

Circuit — yes, guaranteed. Packet — no, shared dynamically. Delay: Circuit — constant, predictable. Packet — variable (queuing delay). Efficiency: Circuit — poor for bursty data. Packet — high efficiency for bursty data. Setup Required: Circuit — yes, before transmission. Packet — no (datagram). Best For: Circuit — voice, real-time audio/video. Packet — data, internet traffic. 3.8 X.25 X.25 is an ITU-T standard for packet-switched wide area network communication, developed in the 1970s.

25 is an ITU-T standard for packet-switched wide area network communication, developed in the 1970s. It was the first international packet-switching standard and dominated WAN connectivity before the Internet era. Protocol Layers: X.25 defines three layers: Physical (X.21), Data Link (LAPB — Link Access Procedure Balanced), and Packet (network layer). Virtual Circuits: X.25 uses both Permanent Virtual Circuits (PVCs) and Switched Virtual Circuits (SVCs). Error Correction: X.

es both Permanent Virtual Circuits (PVCs) and Switched Virtual Circuits (SVCs). Error Correction: X.25 provides extensive error detection and correction at every node — designed for noisy analog telephone lines of the 1970s. Addressing: uses X.121 addressing (up to 15-digit DTE address). Speed: typically 9.6kbps – 2Mbps. Overhead: heavy error correction and flow control at each hop makes X.25 slow by modern standards. Legacy: largely replaced by Frame Relay and IP networks.

ch hop makes X.25 slow by modern standards. Legacy: largely replaced by Frame Relay and IP networks. Still used in some ATM and credit card payment systems. 3.9 Frame Relay Frame Relay is a streamlined, high-performance packet-switching protocol for WAN communication. It is a simplified, faster successor to X.25, removing the extensive per-hop error correction (relying on end-to-end error correction instead). Virtual Circuits: operates over Permanent Virtual Circuits (PVCs).

to-end error correction instead). Virtual Circuits: operates over Permanent Virtual Circuits (PVCs). Uses DLCI (Data Link Connection Identifier) to identify VCs. CIR — Committed Information Rate: the guaranteed bandwidth for a VC. Traffic above CIR may be sent (tagged as DE — Discard Eligible) but can be dropped if network is congested. Congestion Control: FECN (Forward Explicit Congestion Notification) and BECN (Backward Explicit Congestion Notification) bits signal congestion to endpoints.

ification) and BECN (Backward Explicit Congestion Notification) bits signal congestion to endpoints. Speed: typically 56kbps – 45Mbps (T3). Much faster than X.25. No Error Correction at Intermediate Nodes: relies on higher-layer protocols (TCP) for end-to-end error recovery. Reduces overhead dramatically. Applications: corporate WAN connectivity, interconnecting LANs across cities. Largely replaced by MPLS and Ethernet WAN.

PART B — SECURITY AND CRYPTOGRAPHY UNIT IV — Attacks on Computers and Computer Security 4.1 Introduction to Computer Security Computer security (also called cybersecurity or IT security) is the protection of computer systems, networks, and data from theft, damage, unauthorised access, disclosure, disruption, or modification.

networks, and data from theft, damage, unauthorised access, disclosure, disruption, or modification. As digital systems have become central to business, government, defence, healthcare, and personal life, the importance of security has grown enormously. Every connected system is potentially vulnerable. 4.

he importance of security has grown enormously. Every connected system is potentially vulnerable. 4.2 The Need for Security Protection of Sensitive Data: personal information, financial records, intellectual property, trade secrets, and national security data must be protected from unauthorised access. Business Continuity: cyberattacks can disrupt operations, causing enormous financial losses. The average cost of a data breach exceeds $4 million (IBM, 2023).

causing enormous financial losses. The average cost of a data breach exceeds $4 million (IBM, 2023). Legal and Regulatory Compliance: GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, IT Act mandate specific security standards with legal penalties for non-compliance. Trust and Reputation: security breaches destroy customer trust and brand reputation — often impossible to fully recover. National Security: critical infrastructure (power grids, water systems, financial systems) are targets of state-sponsored cyberattacks.

ructure (power grids, water systems, financial systems) are targets of state-sponsored cyberattacks. Prevention of Financial Crime: fraud, ransomware, and theft cause trillions of dollars in global losses annually. 4.3 Security Approaches 4.3.1 Prevention Access Control: restricting who can access systems and data — authentication, authorisation, least privilege principle. Encryption: transforming data so only authorised parties can read it.

on, least privilege principle. Encryption: transforming data so only authorised parties can read it. Firewalls: filtering network traffic based on rules to block malicious packets. Patch Management: regularly updating software to fix known vulnerabilities. Security Training: educating users to recognise and avoid social engineering and phishing attacks. 4.3.2 Detection Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS): monitor network/system activity for signs of attacks or policy violations.

Detection Systems (IDS): monitor network/system activity for signs of attacks or policy violations. Security Information and Event Management (SIEM): aggregate and analyse security events from across the organisation. Audit Logs: comprehensive logging of system and user activities for forensic analysis. Vulnerability Scanning: automated tools scan systems for known vulnerabilities. 4.3.

Business Continuity Plan (BCP): ensures critical operations can continue after a security incident. Disaster Recovery: restoring systems and data after a major incident.

nue after a security incident. Disaster Recovery: restoring systems and data after a major incident. Forensic Analysis: post-incident investigation to understand what happened and prevent recurrence. 4.4 Principles of Security (CIA Triad + Extensions) Confidentiality: ensuring information is accessible only to those authorised to have access. Violated by unauthorised disclosure, eavesdropping, or data theft. Enforced by encryption, access controls.

d by unauthorised disclosure, eavesdropping, or data theft. Enforced by encryption, access controls. Integrity: ensuring accuracy and completeness of information — protection from unauthorised modification. Violated by data corruption, malware, or man-in-the-middle attacks. Enforced by hashing, digital signatures, access controls. Availability: ensuring authorised users have reliable, timely access to information and systems. Violated by DDoS attacks, ransomware, hardware failures.

, timely access to information and systems. Violated by DDoS attacks, ransomware, hardware failures. Enforced by redundancy, backups, DDoS protection. Authenticity: verifying the identity of communicating parties — the message is genuinely from who it claims. Enforced by digital signatures, certificates, multi-factor authentication. Non-repudiation: preventing parties from denying actions they have performed. Enforced by digital signatures and audit logs — provides legal accountability.

they have performed. Enforced by digital signatures and audit logs — provides legal accountability. Accountability: tracking and logging actions so individuals can be held responsible for their actions on a system. Privacy: protecting personally identifiable information (PII) and controlling how it is collected, used, and shared. 4.5 Types of Attacks 4.5.1 Passive Attacks Passive attacks involve monitoring or eavesdropping on communications without altering them.

Attacks Passive attacks involve monitoring or eavesdropping on communications without altering them. The attacker gathers information without the victim knowing. Hard to detect since no data is changed. Eavesdropping (Sniffing): capturing network packets passing over a network segment. Tools: Wireshark, tcpdump. Threat to confidentiality. Traffic Analysis: even if traffic is encrypted, analysing patterns (who communicates with whom, when, how much) can reveal sensitive information.

d, analysing patterns (who communicates with whom, when, how much) can reveal sensitive information. Network Scanning: probing a network to discover hosts, open ports, and services (reconnaissance). Tools: Nmap. 4.5.2 Active Attacks Active attacks involve modifying data, creating false data streams, or disrupting services. They directly affect the target system. Masquerade (Spoofing): attacker pretends to be a legitimate user or system. IP spoofing, email spoofing, caller ID spoofing.

ttacker pretends to be a legitimate user or system. IP spoofing, email spoofing, caller ID spoofing. Replay Attack: attacker captures legitimate authentication data and retransmits it later to gain unauthorised access. Countered by timestamps and nonces. Man-in-the-Middle (MITM): attacker inserts themselves between two communicating parties, intercepting and possibly altering messages. Countered by PKI and certificate pinning.

ting parties, intercepting and possibly altering messages. Countered by PKI and certificate pinning. Modification of Messages: legitimate messages are altered, delayed, or reordered by an attacker. Denial of Service (DoS): attacker floods target with traffic or requests, exhausting resources and making the service unavailable to legitimate users. Distributed DoS (DDoS): DoS using a botnet — thousands of compromised machines (zombies) simultaneously attack the target.

: DoS using a botnet — thousands of compromised machines (zombies) simultaneously attack the target. Much harder to defend against. 4.5.3 Software-Based Attacks Malware (Malicious Software): umbrella term for software designed to harm. Virus: self-replicating code that attaches to legitimate files and executes when the host program runs. Spreads via file sharing. Worm: self-replicating malware that spreads autonomously across networks without needing a host file. E.g., WannaCry, Conficker.

re that spreads autonomously across networks without needing a host file. E.g., WannaCry, Conficker. Trojan Horse: malicious code hidden inside seemingly legitimate software. Does not self-replicate. Creates backdoors, steals data. Ransomware: encrypts victim's files and demands payment (cryptocurrency) for decryption key. E.g., WannaCry, REvil, LockBit. Spyware / Keylogger: secretly monitors user activities and collects keystrokes, passwords, and screenshots.

/ Keylogger: secretly monitors user activities and collects keystrokes, passwords, and screenshots. Rootkit: gains root/admin level access and hides itself and other malware from detection. Very hard to remove. Botnet: network of malware-infected computers (bots/zombies) controlled remotely by attacker (C&C server). Used for DDoS, spam, credential stuffing. Adware: displays unwanted advertisements; may track browsing behaviour. 4.5.

Adware: displays unwanted advertisements; may track browsing behaviour. 4.5.4 Social Engineering Attacks Phishing: fraudulent emails impersonating legitimate entities to trick users into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links. Spear Phishing: highly targeted phishing against specific individuals or organisations using personalised information. Whaling: phishing targeting senior executives (CEO, CFO).

anisations using personalised information. Whaling: phishing targeting senior executives (CEO, CFO). Vishing: voice phishing — fraudulent phone calls posing as banks, technical support, or government agencies. Smishing: SMS phishing — fraudulent text messages with malicious links. Pretexting: attacker fabricates a scenario (pretext) to manipulate victim into providing information or access. Baiting: leaving infected USB drives in public places hoping someone will plug them in.

n or access. Baiting: leaving infected USB drives in public places hoping someone will plug them in. Tailgating / Piggybacking: physical security breach — following an authorised person into a restricted area. 4.5.5 Web Application Attacks SQL Injection: malicious SQL queries injected through web forms to access, modify, or delete database contents. Countered by parameterised queries. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): malicious scripts injected into web pages viewed by other users.
