UNIT V — Cryptography: Concepts and Techniques 5.1 Introduction to Cryptography Cryptography is the science and art of securing information through mathematical techniques. It transforms readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable form (ciphertext) using an algorithm and a key, ensuring that only authorised parties can read the original data.

ext) using an algorithm and a key, ensuring that only authorised parties can read the original data. Cryptanalysis: the art of breaking cryptographic systems — analysing ciphers to recover plaintext without the key. Cryptology: the combined study of cryptography and cryptanalysis. Steganography vs Cryptography: cryptography hides the content of a message; steganography hides the existence of a message.

graphy: cryptography hides the content of a message; steganography hides the existence of a message. Kerckhoffs's Principle: a cryptographic system should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is public knowledge. 5.2 Plain Text and Cipher Text Plaintext (P): original, readable data before encryption. Also called cleartext. Ciphertext (C): encrypted, unintelligible data produced by applying an encryption algorithm to plaintext.

rtext (C): encrypted, unintelligible data produced by applying an encryption algorithm to plaintext. Encryption (E): process: C = E(K, P) — applying cipher algorithm E with key K to plaintext P to produce ciphertext C. Decryption (D): process: P = D(K, C) — applying decryption algorithm D with key K to ciphertext C to recover plaintext P. Key (K): secret parameter that controls the encryption/decryption process. Security depends entirely on the key, not the algorithm.

controls the encryption/decryption process. Security depends entirely on the key, not the algorithm. Key Space: the set of all possible keys. Larger key space = harder to brute-force. 5.3 Symmetric Key Cryptography In symmetric key (secret key / private key) cryptography, the same key is used for both encryption and decryption. Both sender and receiver must share the same secret key securely. Advantage: very fast — suitable for encrypting large amounts of data.

the same secret key securely. Advantage: very fast — suitable for encrypting large amounts of data. Disadvantage: key distribution problem — how to securely share the key? With N users, N(N-1)/2 keys needed. Types: Stream Ciphers (encrypt bit-by-bit) and Block Ciphers (encrypt fixed-size blocks). 5.3.1 Stream Ciphers Operation: encrypts one bit or byte at a time by XORing plaintext with a keystream generated from the key.

ion: encrypts one bit or byte at a time by XORing plaintext with a keystream generated from the key. Keystream: pseudorandom bit sequence generated from the key using a PRNG (Pseudo-Random Number Generator). XOR Operation: ciphertext bit = plaintext bit XOR keystream bit. Same operation for decryption. Examples: RC4 (now deprecated), ChaCha20 (used in TLS 1.3), A5/1 (GSM voice encryption). Use Cases: real-time communication (voice/video), situations where data arrives continuously. 5.3.

. Use Cases: real-time communication (voice/video), situations where data arrives continuously. 5.3.2 Block Ciphers Block ciphers divide plaintext into fixed-size blocks and encrypt each block. If plaintext is not a multiple of block size, padding is added. ECB — Electronic Code Book: simplest mode. Each block encrypted independently with the same key. Problem: identical plaintext blocks → identical ciphertext blocks (patterns visible). NOT secure.

y. Problem: identical plaintext blocks → identical ciphertext blocks (patterns visible). NOT secure. CBC — Cipher Block Chaining: each plaintext block XORed with previous ciphertext block before encryption. Requires an Initialisation Vector (IV) for the first block. Error propagation. Most widely used. CFB — Cipher FeedBack: converts block cipher to stream cipher. Previous ciphertext fed back into cipher. Suitable for streaming data.

lock cipher to stream cipher. Previous ciphertext fed back into cipher. Suitable for streaming data. OFB — Output FeedBack: keystream generated independently of plaintext/ciphertext. No error propagation. Good for noisy channels. CTR — Counter Mode: encrypts a counter value for each block. Parallelisable — excellent performance. Used in modern protocols. GCM — Galois/Counter Mode: CTR mode + authentication (GHASH). Provides both confidentiality and integrity. Used in TLS 1.3, AES-GCM. 5.

+ authentication (GHASH). Provides both confidentiality and integrity. Used in TLS 1.3, AES-GCM. 5.4 Data Encryption Standard (DES) DES is a symmetric block cipher adopted as a US federal standard in 1977. It was the dominant encryption standard for two decades. Block Size: 64 bits (8 bytes). Key Size: 56 bits effective (64-bit key with 8 parity bits). Rounds: 16 rounds of Feistel network processing. Feistel Structure: each round: splits 64-bit block into L (left) and R (right) halves.

k processing. Feistel Structure: each round: splits 64-bit block into L (left) and R (right) halves. R goes through F-function (expansion, XOR with subkey, S-box substitution, permutation). New R = old L XOR F(R, subkey). New L = old R. S-Boxes (Substitution Boxes): 8 S-boxes, each taking 6 input bits and outputting 4 bits. The only non-linear component — provides confusion. P-Boxes (Permutation): rearrange bit positions. Provide diffusion.

r component — provides confusion. P-Boxes (Permutation): rearrange bit positions. Provide diffusion. Key Schedule: generates 16 48-bit subkeys from the 56-bit master key using permutations and circular shifts. Weakness: 56-bit key is too short. In 1998, EFF's Deep Crack broke DES in 56 hours. In 1999, DES cracked in 22 hours. DES is now insecure. 3DES (Triple DES): applies DES three times: C = E(K3, D(K2, E(K1, P))). Effective key size 112 or 168 bits. Slow but backward compatible.

times: C = E(K3, D(K2, E(K1, P))). Effective key size 112 or 168 bits. Slow but backward compatible. Being phased out. 5.5 International Data Encryption Algorithm (IDEA) IDEA is a symmetric block cipher designed by Xuejia Lai and James Massey in 1991 as a replacement for DES. Block Size: 64 bits. Key Size: 128 bits — much larger than DES, providing far greater security. Rounds: 8 identical rounds plus a final output transformation.

DES, providing far greater security. Rounds: 8 identical rounds plus a final output transformation. Operations: uses three algebraic operations: XOR (⊕), addition modulo 2^16 (+), and multiplication modulo (2^16 + 1) (⊗). The mixing of these three incompatible operations provides strong security. Subkeys: generates 52 16-bit subkeys from the 128-bit key. Strengths: no known cryptanalytic weaknesses. Strong and secure. No weak keys (unlike DES).

bit key. Strengths: no known cryptanalytic weaknesses. Strong and secure. No weak keys (unlike DES). Applications: used in PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) email encryption, SSL (older versions). Status: IDEA's patents expired in 2012, now freely available. Being replaced by AES in modern systems. 5.6 Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) AES (Rijndael) is the current US federal standard for symmetric encryption, adopted by NIST in 2001 as a replacement for DES/3DES.

US federal standard for symmetric encryption, adopted by NIST in 2001 as a replacement for DES/3DES. It is the most widely used symmetric cipher in the world today. Block Size: 128 bits (fixed). Key Sizes: 128 bits (10 rounds), 192 bits (12 rounds), or 256 bits (14 rounds). Structure: Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN) — NOT a Feistel cipher. State Array: data organised as a 4×4 matrix of bytes (128-bit block). 5.6.1 AES Round Operations SubBytes: non-linear substitution step.

matrix of bytes (128-bit block). 5.6.1 AES Round Operations SubBytes: non-linear substitution step. Each byte replaced using a fixed S-Box (constructed from finite field inverses). Provides confusion. ShiftRows: bytes in each row of the state matrix cyclically shifted left by 0, 1, 2, 3 positions. Provides diffusion across columns. MixColumns: each column treated as a polynomial over GF(2^8) and multiplied by a fixed matrix. Mixes bytes within columns. Provides diffusion (except last round).

nd multiplied by a fixed matrix. Mixes bytes within columns. Provides diffusion (except last round). AddRoundKey: XOR state with the round key (derived from key schedule). The only key addition step. 5.6.2 AES Key Schedule Generates (Nr + 1) round keys from the original key using recursive expansion, SubWord (S-Box substitution), RotWord (rotation), and Rcon (round constant) XOR. Security: no known practical attacks against correctly implemented AES.

d Rcon (round constant) XOR. Security: no known practical attacks against correctly implemented AES. The best known attack (related-key attack on AES-256) requires 2^119 computations — astronomically infeasible. Performance: hardware acceleration via AES-NI CPU instructions enables >10Gbps throughput on modern processors. Applications: TLS/HTTPS, WiFi (WPA2/WPA3), disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS), VPNs, government communications. 5.

WiFi (WPA2/WPA3), disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS), VPNs, government communications. 5.7 Asymmetric Key Cryptography Asymmetric (public-key) cryptography uses a mathematically related pair of keys: a public key (shared freely) and a private key (kept secret). Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the corresponding private key, and vice versa. Key Pair: Public Key (PK) and Private Key (SK). Computationally infeasible to derive SK from PK.

sa. Key Pair: Public Key (PK) and Private Key (SK). Computationally infeasible to derive SK from PK. Encryption: sender encrypts with recipient's PUBLIC key. Only recipient can decrypt with their PRIVATE key. Digital Signature: sender signs with their PRIVATE key. Anyone can verify with sender's PUBLIC key. Advantages: solves key distribution problem — public key can be published openly. Enables non-repudiation. Disadvantages: 10,000–1,000,000× slower than symmetric encryption.

openly. Enables non-repudiation. Disadvantages: 10,000–1,000,000× slower than symmetric encryption. Not suitable for bulk data encryption. Hybrid Encryption: TLS/SSL use asymmetric crypto to securely exchange a symmetric session key, then use symmetric crypto for bulk data. 5.8 RSA Algorithm RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman, 1977) is the most widely used asymmetric cipher. Its security is based on the difficulty of factoring large integers (the product of two large primes). 5.8.

urity is based on the difficulty of factoring large integers (the product of two large primes). 5.8.1 RSA Key Generation Step 1: choose two large prime numbers p and q (each 1024–4096 bits). Step 2: compute n = p × q (the modulus). n is public. Step 3: compute Euler's totient: φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1). Step 4: choose e such that 1 < e < φ(n) and gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1. Common choice: e = 65537. Step 5: compute d = e⁻¹ mod φ(n) (modular multiplicative inverse). Public Key: (n, e). Private Key: (n, d).

compute d = e⁻¹ mod φ(n) (modular multiplicative inverse). Public Key: (n, e). Private Key: (n, d). p, q, and φ(n) must be kept secret or destroyed. 5.8.2 RSA Encryption and Decryption Encryption: C = M^e mod n — raise plaintext M to power e, modulo n. Decryption: M = C^d mod n — raise ciphertext C to power d, modulo n. Signing: Signature S = H(M)^d mod n — sign hash of message with private key.

C to power d, modulo n. Signing: Signature S = H(M)^d mod n — sign hash of message with private key. Verification: H(M) = S^e mod n — verify by decrypting signature with public key and comparing to hash. 5.8.3 RSA Security Key Sizes: RSA-1024 considered broken; RSA-2048 current minimum; RSA-4096 for high security. Vulnerability to Quantum: Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer can factor RSA keys in polynomial time.

erability to Quantum: Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer can factor RSA keys in polynomial time. Post-quantum alternatives: lattice-based cryptography (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium). Padding: raw RSA is deterministic (same message → same ciphertext). OAEP and PKCS#1 v2.2 padding are required for security. 5.9 Digital Signatures A digital signature is an asymmetric cryptographic mechanism that provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation for digital documents and messages. 5.

that provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation for digital documents and messages. 5.9.1 How Digital Signatures Work Step 1 — Hash: sender computes a cryptographic hash (SHA-256) of the message: h = H(M). Step 2 — Sign: sender encrypts hash with their PRIVATE key: S = E(SK_sender, h). S is the digital signature. Step 3 — Transmit: sender transmits message M + signature S. Step 4 — Verify: receiver decrypts signature with sender's PUBLIC key to get h': h' = D(PK_sender, S).

ep 4 — Verify: receiver decrypts signature with sender's PUBLIC key to get h': h' = D(PK_sender, S). Step 5 — Compare: receiver computes H(M) independently and compares to h'. If equal — signature is valid, message is authentic and unmodified. Authentication: only the private key owner could have created the signature — proves identity. Integrity: any modification to the message invalidates the signature. Non-repudiation: signer cannot deny signing — private key is unique to them.

Unlike cryptography (which makes content unreadable), steganography hides the very existence of the secret message. 5.10.

which makes content unreadable), steganography hides the very existence of the secret message. 5.10.1 Digital Steganography Techniques LSB (Least Significant Bit) Insertion: the least significant bit of each pixel in an image (or sample in audio) is replaced with a bit of the secret message. Imperceptible to human eyes/ears but detectable by steganalysis tools. Masking and Filtering: hide information in more significant bits (more visible changes). More robust to lossy compression.

hide information in more significant bits (more visible changes). More robust to lossy compression. Transform Domain: hide information in frequency domain coefficients (DCT coefficients in JPEG, DWT in images). More resistant to steganalysis. Text Steganography: hiding data in text documents using whitespace, synonym substitution, or formatting. Network Steganography: hiding data in unused header fields of network protocols (e.g., TCP/IP header fields).

teganography: hiding data in unused header fields of network protocols (e.g., TCP/IP header fields). Spread Spectrum: spread secret signal across a wide frequency range at very low power — resembles noise. Carrier (Cover Object): the innocent-looking file containing the hidden message — image, audio, video, text. Stego Object: carrier with hidden message embedded. Stego Key: key used to embed and extract the hidden message.

: carrier with hidden message embedded. Stego Key: key used to embed and extract the hidden message. Steganalysis: the art of detecting steganography — statistical analysis of carrier to find anomalies. Applications: digital watermarking, covert communication, copyright protection, leak detection. UNIT VI — Internet Security Protocols 6.1 Introduction and Basic Concepts Internet security protocols are standardised frameworks that provide security services (confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation) for communications over the Internet. They are implemented at various layers of the TCP/IP stack. Layer 2 Security: WPA2/WPA3 (Wi-Fi), 802.1X (port-based access control). Layer 3 Security: IPSec — encrypts IP packets.

PA2/WPA3 (Wi-Fi), 802.1X (port-based access control). Layer 3 Security: IPSec — encrypts IP packets. Layer 4/Session Security: SSL/TLS — most widely deployed internet security protocol. Application Layer Security: S/MIME (email), PGP, SSH, DNSSEC. 6.2 Secure Socket Layer (SSL) SSL was developed by Netscape in 1994-1996 to provide security for HTTP communications. SSL 3.0 was the last version before TLS replaced it. SSL is now deprecated due to vulnerabilities (POODLE attack).

e last version before TLS replaced it. SSL is now deprecated due to vulnerabilities (POODLE attack). SSL Architecture: sits between the Application layer (HTTP) and Transport layer (TCP). Two sub-protocols: SSL Record Protocol (data transfer) and SSL Handshake Protocol (negotiation). SSL Record Protocol: takes application data, fragments it, optionally compresses, adds MAC (integrity), encrypts, and adds SSL header.

tion data, fragments it, optionally compresses, adds MAC (integrity), encrypts, and adds SSL header. SSL Handshake Protocol: negotiates cipher suite, authenticates server (and optionally client), and establishes shared session keys. SSL Handshake Steps (simplified): (1) ClientHello — proposes cipher suites. (2) ServerHello — selects cipher suite. (3) Certificate — server sends certificate. (4) Key exchange. (5) ChangeCipherSpec + Finished. Vulnerabilities: POODLE (SSL 3.

TLS Handshake (TLS 1.3): (1) ClientHello (supported ciphers, key shares). (2) ServerHello + Certificate + Finished. (3) Client Finished. Encrypted data can flow after step 2.

ange means each session uses a unique key. Compromise of long-term key cannot decrypt past sessions. Standard for all web security. Port 443. Server presents TLS certificate. All traffic encrypted end-to-end. Indicated by padlock in browser.

Server presents TLS certificate. All traffic encrypted end-to-end. Indicated by padlock in browser. ch secures the TCP connection), SHTTP secures individual HTTP messages. Largely superseded by HTTPS. A timestamp proves a document existed at or before a specific point in time. Process: (1) Client computes hash of document.

cument existed at or before a specific point in time. Process: (1) Client computes hash of document. (2) Client sends hash to TSA. (3) TSA creates a timestamp token: TST = sign(hash || current_time) with TSA's private key. (4) Client receives and stores TST. igital signatures (proving certificate was valid when signed), timestamping blockchain transactions. 9) as a comprehensive security protocol specifically for credit card transactions over the Internet. tion. Cardholder signs ORDER INFO for merchant + PAYMENT INFO for bank with a single dual signature. Merchant sees order info but not card details. Bank sees payment info but not order details. UNIT VII — User Authentication and Kerberos 7.1 Introduction to Authentication Authentication is the process of verifying the claimed identity of a user, process, or device. It is the first step in access control — you must prove who you are before you can be granted access. Identification: claiming an identity — presenting a username or ID. Who are you? Authentication: proving that claimed identity — providing evidence.

g a username or ID. Who are you? Authentication: proving that claimed identity — providing evidence. Prove it! Authorisation: determining what an authenticated identity is allowed to do. What can you access? Accounting (Auditing): recording what an authenticated user did. What did you do? AAA Framework: Authentication, Authorisation, Accounting — fundamental security framework. 7.2 Authentication Basics 7.2.

tication, Authorisation, Accounting — fundamental security framework. 7.2 Authentication Basics 7.2.1 Three Factors of Authentication Something You Know (Knowledge Factor): passwords, PINs, security questions, passphrases. Something You Have (Possession Factor): smart cards, hardware tokens (RSA SecurID), OTP devices, mobile phone (for SMS OTP). Something You Are (Inherence Factor): biometrics — fingerprint, iris scan, face recognition, voice recognition, retina scan.

ence Factor): biometrics — fingerprint, iris scan, face recognition, voice recognition, retina scan. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): combining two or more factors. Even if one factor is compromised, attacker still needs the others. 2FA (two-factor) is the most common MFA implementation. 7.3 Passwords Passwords are the most common authentication mechanism — despite being the weakest. A password is a secret string known only to the user. 7.3.

on mechanism — despite being the weakest. A password is a secret string known only to the user. 7.3.1 Password Vulnerabilities Brute Force Attack: trying all possible combinations. Mitigated by long complex passwords and account lockout. Dictionary Attack: trying common words and variations. Mitigated by enforcing non-dictionary passwords. Rainbow Table Attack: precomputed hash-to-password lookup tables. Mitigated by salted hashing.

ords. Rainbow Table Attack: precomputed hash-to-password lookup tables. Mitigated by salted hashing. Phishing: tricking users into entering credentials on fake sites. Credential Stuffing: using username/password pairs from one breach to attack other services (password reuse). Shoulder Surfing: physically observing password entry. Keylogging: malware capturing keystrokes. 7.3.2 Password Storage — Hashing with Salt Never store plaintext passwords: breach exposes all passwords immediately.

orage — Hashing with Salt Never store plaintext passwords: breach exposes all passwords immediately. Hash the password: store H(password). Verify by hashing entered password and comparing. Problem: dictionary/rainbow table attacks. Salt: random per-user value appended to password before hashing: H(salt || password). Stored with hash. Makes rainbow tables useless.

ded to password before hashing: H(salt || password). Stored with hash. Makes rainbow tables useless. Modern algorithms: bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2 (winner of Password Hashing Competition) — intentionally slow (key-stretching) to slow brute force attacks. Not MD5 or SHA-1 (too fast). Pepper: server-side secret added to all passwords — protects if database is stolen but application code is not. 7.

-side secret added to all passwords — protects if database is stolen but application code is not. 7.4 Authentication Tokens Authentication tokens are physical devices or software applications that generate one-time passwords (OTPs) or cryptographic proofs. 7.4.1 Types of Tokens Hardware OTP Token (Time-Based): generates a 6-8 digit TOTP (Time-based OTP) every 30 seconds using HMAC-SHA1(secret, time). E.g., RSA SecurID, YubiKey OTP.

OTP (Time-based OTP) every 30 seconds using HMAC-SHA1(secret, time). E.g., RSA SecurID, YubiKey OTP. Software Token / Authenticator App: same TOTP algorithm in a smartphone app. E.g., Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy. HMAC-based OTP (HOTP): counter-based OTP. Each button press generates a new OTP by incrementing a counter. Smart Card: credit-card-sized device with embedded microprocessor and cryptographic key. Requires PIN to activate. Used in CAC, PIV, banking EMV cards.

microprocessor and cryptographic key. Requires PIN to activate. Used in CAC, PIV, banking EMV cards. FIDO2 / WebAuthn Hardware Key: phishing-resistant hardware key (e.g., YubiKey, Titan Security Key) using public-key cryptography. No shared secrets — private key never leaves device. Push Notification: authentication app (Duo, Okta) sends push notification to registered phone. User approves/denies. SMS OTP: one-time password sent via SMS.

ush notification to registered phone. User approves/denies. SMS OTP: one-time password sent via SMS. Widely used but vulnerable to SIM swapping and SS7 attacks — not recommended for high-security applications. 7.5 Certificate-Based Authentication Certificate-based authentication uses X.509 digital certificates to prove identity, relying on the trust hierarchy of a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI).

certificates to prove identity, relying on the trust hierarchy of a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). Process: user presents digital certificate + proves possession of corresponding private key (signs a challenge). Server validates certificate against trusted CA. TLS Client Authentication: mutual TLS (mTLS) — both client and server present certificates. Used in high-security B2B APIs, corporate VPNs, IoT device authentication.

ver present certificates. Used in high-security B2B APIs, corporate VPNs, IoT device authentication. Certificate-based SSH: SSH keys (public/private key pairs) stored in ssh_authorized_keys. Preferred over password authentication. SAML / OAuth / OIDC: federated authentication standards — user authenticates once with Identity Provider (IdP), gets token for Service Providers. Single Sign-On (SSO). 7.6 Kerberos Kerberos is a network authentication protocol developed at MIT.

. Single Sign-On (SSO). 7.6 Kerberos Kerberos is a network authentication protocol developed at MIT. It provides strong mutual authentication for client-server applications using secret-key cryptography. Named after the three-headed dog guarding Hades in Greek mythology. Used in Microsoft Active Directory, Unix/Linux (via SSSD), and many enterprise environments. 7.6.1 Kerberos Components Client: the user or service requesting access.

ny enterprise environments. 7.6.1 Kerberos Components Client: the user or service requesting access. Application Server (AS): the resource the client wants to access (file server, database, etc.). Key Distribution Center (KDC): the trusted third party. Consists of two components: Authentication Server (AS) and Ticket-Granting Server (TGS). Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT): encrypted ticket issued by KDC-AS. Proves the client has authenticated to the KDC.

ing Ticket (TGT): encrypted ticket issued by KDC-AS. Proves the client has authenticated to the KDC. Used to request service tickets without re-authenticating. Service Ticket (ST): ticket issued by KDC-TGS authorising access to a specific application server. 7.6.2 Kerberos Authentication Process Step 1 — AS-REQ: client sends username to Authentication Server (AS). Client does NOT send password.

Step 1 — AS-REQ: client sends username to Authentication Server (AS). Client does NOT send password. Step 2 — AS-REP: AS verifies user exists, generates: TGT (encrypted with KDC-TGS secret key) + session key (encrypted with client's key derived from password). Client decrypts its portion using their password-derived key. Step 3 — TGS-REQ: client presents TGT + authenticator (timestamp encrypted with session key) to TGS, requesting access to specific service.

authenticator (timestamp encrypted with session key) to TGS, requesting access to specific service. Step 4 — TGS-REP: TGS validates TGT and authenticator. Issues Service Ticket (encrypted with application server's key) + new session key for client-server communication. Step 5 — AP-REQ: client presents Service Ticket + new authenticator to Application Server. Step 6 — AP-REP (optional): Application Server decrypts service ticket, validates authenticator.

er. Step 6 — AP-REP (optional): Application Server decrypts service ticket, validates authenticator. Optionally sends back AP-REP to prove it has the correct key (mutual authentication). 7.6.3 Kerberos Security Properties Passwords never traverse the network: authentication uses challenge-response based on keys derived from password. Mutual Authentication: both client and server verify each other's identity.

s derived from password. Mutual Authentication: both client and server verify each other's identity. Single Sign-On (SSO): one login grants TGT for accessing multiple services without re-authentication. Ticket Expiry: TGTs typically valid for 8–10 hours. Limits window for ticket theft. Replay Protection: timestamps and authenticators prevent replay attacks. Clock synchronisation required (typically within 5 minutes — uses NTP). 7.6.

prevent replay attacks. Clock synchronisation required (typically within 5 minutes — uses NTP). 7.6.4 Kerberos Vulnerabilities Pass-the-Ticket: attacker steals a valid Kerberos ticket from memory (using Mimikatz) and uses it to authenticate. Golden Ticket Attack: attacker obtains KRBTGT hash (KDC's long-term key) and forges arbitrary TGTs — can impersonate any user for 10+ years.

RBTGT hash (KDC's long-term key) and forges arbitrary TGTs — can impersonate any user for 10+ years. Kerberoasting: requesting service tickets for service accounts, then offline brute-forcing the ticket to recover service account password. AS-REP Roasting: for accounts with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication', request TGT and crack password offline. 7.7 Key Distribution Center (KDC) The KDC is the central trusted authority in the Kerberos system.

. 7.7 Key Distribution Center (KDC) The KDC is the central trusted authority in the Kerberos system. It maintains a database of all principals (users and services) and their associated long-term secret keys. Authentication Server (AS) Component: handles initial authentication. Verifies user identity and issues TGTs. Ticket-Granting Server (TGS) Component: handles service ticket requests. Validates TGTs and issues service tickets.

Server (TGS) Component: handles service ticket requests. Validates TGTs and issues service tickets. KRBTGT Account: special service account whose key is used to encrypt/sign all TGTs. Extremely sensitive — compromise = golden ticket attack. KDC Security: the KDC is the most critical component in Kerberos. It must be physically and logically secured. Multiple KDCs with database replication provide redundancy. Realm: administrative domain managed by a single KDC.

with database replication provide redundancy. Realm: administrative domain managed by a single KDC. Cross-realm authentication allows users from one Kerberos realm to access services in another. 7.8 Digital Certificates and X.509 A digital certificate is an electronic document that binds a public key to an identity (person, organisation, server). Issued and digitally signed by a Certificate Authority (CA). 7.8.1 X.509 Certificate Structure Version: X.509v3 (current). Supports extensions.

e Authority (CA). 7.8.1 X.509 Certificate Structure Version: X.509v3 (current). Supports extensions. Serial Number: unique identifier assigned by the CA. Signature Algorithm: algorithm used by CA to sign the certificate (e.g., sha256WithRSAEncryption). Issuer: Distinguished Name (DN) of the CA that issued the certificate. Validity Period: Not Before and Not After dates. Subject: DN of the entity the certificate belongs to. For TLS: includes Common Name (CN) = domain name.

ject: DN of the entity the certificate belongs to. For TLS: includes Common Name (CN) = domain name. Subject Public Key Info: the public key algorithm and the public key value. Extensions (v3): Subject Alternative Names (SANs — additional domain names), Key Usage, Extended Key Usage, CRL Distribution Points, OCSP URL. CA's Digital Signature: CA's signature over all the above fields. What makes the certificate trustworthy. 7.8.2 Certificate Trust Hierarchy (PKI) Root CA: self-signed certificate.

e certificate trustworthy. 7.8.2 Certificate Trust Hierarchy (PKI) Root CA: self-signed certificate. Ultimate trust anchor. Root CAs are pre-installed in OS and browser trust stores. Kept offline for security. Intermediate CA: signed by Root CA. Issues end-entity certificates. Allows Root CA to stay offline. End-Entity Certificate: issued to server, user, or device. Contains subject's public key. Certificate Chain Validation: browser validates each certificate in the chain up to a trusted root.

Certificate Chain Validation: browser validates each certificate in the chain up to a trusted root. Checks signatures, validity dates, and revocation status. 7.8.3 Certificate Revocation CRL (Certificate Revocation List): list of revoked certificate serial numbers published by CA. Downloaded periodically — can be stale. OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol): real-time certificate revocation check. Client queries OCSP responder with certificate serial number.

eal-time certificate revocation check. Client queries OCSP responder with certificate serial number. OCSP Stapling improves performance. Reasons for Revocation: key compromise, CA compromise, affiliation change, superseded, cessation of operation. 7.9 Private Key Management Hardware Security Module (HSM): tamper-resistant hardware device for generating, storing, and using cryptographic keys. Keys never leave HSM in plaintext. Used by banks, CAs, payment processors.

using cryptographic keys. Keys never leave HSM in plaintext. Used by banks, CAs, payment processors. Key Generation: keys must be generated using a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator (CSPRNG). True random number generators (TRNG) based on physical entropy preferred for long-term keys. Key Storage: private keys should be stored encrypted (protected by passphrase or HSM). Never stored in plaintext files.

e keys should be stored encrypted (protected by passphrase or HSM). Never stored in plaintext files. Key Escrow: encrypted backup of private keys with a trusted third party for recovery. Controversial — creates additional attack surface. Key Rotation: periodically replacing keys limits exposure if a key is compromised. Key Destruction: when a key is no longer needed, it must be securely destroyed — cryptographic erase (overwrite with random data multiple times). PKCS#12 (.pfx/.

ecurely destroyed — cryptographic erase (overwrite with random data multiple times). PKCS#12 (.pfx/.p12): standard format for storing private key + certificate together, protected by password. PKCS#8: standard format for storing private key alone. 7.10 Public Key Cryptography Standards (PKCS) PKCS is a group of public-key cryptography standards developed by RSA Security. They define formats and protocols for various cryptographic operations.

s developed by RSA Security. They define formats and protocols for various cryptographic operations. PKCS#1: RSA cryptography standard — defines RSA key format and padding schemes (PKCS#1 v1.5, OAEP). PKCS#3: Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement Standard. PKCS#5: Password-Based Cryptography — PBKDF2 for deriving keys from passwords. PKCS#7 (CMS): Cryptographic Message Syntax — format for signed and encrypted messages. Used in S/MIME.

CS#7 (CMS): Cryptographic Message Syntax — format for signed and encrypted messages. Used in S/MIME. PKCS#8: Private-Key Information Syntax — standard for storing private keys. PKCS#10: Certificate Request Syntax — format for CSR (Certificate Signing Request) sent to CA. PKCS#11: Cryptographic Token Interface Standard (Cryptoki) — API for interacting with HSMs and smart cards. UNIT VIII — Network Security, Firewalls, and Virtual Private Networks (VPN) 8.1 Introduction Network security encompasses the policies, practices, and technologies designed to prevent, detect, and respond to unauthorised access, misuse, modification, or denial of computer networks and their resources. It requires a layered defence-in-depth approach — no single mechanism is sufficient. 8.

r resources. It requires a layered defence-in-depth approach — no single mechanism is sufficient. 8.2 Brief Introduction to TCP/IP (Security Perspective) The TCP/IP protocol suite was designed for reliability and interoperability, not security. Understanding its structure is essential for understanding network security vulnerabilities. IP Spoofing: TCP/IP does not inherently authenticate the source IP address — attackers can forge the source IP of packets.

es not inherently authenticate the source IP address — attackers can forge the source IP of packets. Countered by ingress/egress filtering (BCP38). TCP SYN Flooding: attacker sends many SYN packets without completing handshakes, exhausting server's connection table (DoS). Countered by SYN cookies. ICMP Attacks: ping flood, Smurf attack (amplification using ICMP broadcast), ICMP redirect poisoning.

CMP Attacks: ping flood, Smurf attack (amplification using ICMP broadcast), ICMP redirect poisoning. ARP Poisoning: sending forged ARP replies to associate attacker's MAC with legitimate IP — enables MITM attacks on local network. DNS Poisoning (Cache Poisoning): inserting forged DNS records into resolver cache — redirects users to malicious sites. DNSSEC prevents this. Port Scanning: probing TCP/UDP ports to discover open services and potential vulnerabilities. Tools: Nmap, Masscan. 8.

bing TCP/UDP ports to discover open services and potential vulnerabilities. Tools: Nmap, Masscan. 8.3 Firewalls A firewall is a network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on predetermined security rules. It establishes a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks. 8.3.1 Types of Firewalls Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless) Operation: inspects each individual packet in isolation.

walls Packet-Filtering Firewall (Stateless) Operation: inspects each individual packet in isolation. Filters based on source/destination IP, source/destination port, protocol, and direction. ACLs (Access Control Lists): rules are evaluated in order; first matching rule applies. Advantages: fast, low overhead, transparent to applications. Disadvantages: cannot track TCP connection state, cannot detect fragmented packet attacks, no application-layer inspection, easily bypassed.

on state, cannot detect fragmented packet attacks, no application-layer inspection, easily bypassed. Example: router ACLs (Cisco IOS access-lists), iptables (Linux). Stateful Inspection Firewall Operation: tracks the state of network connections (state table/connection tracking). Understands whether a packet is part of an established connection, new connection, or invalid. State Table: stores source IP/port, destination IP/port, protocol, connection state (SYN-SENT, ESTABLISHED, etc.).

tores source IP/port, destination IP/port, protocol, connection state (SYN-SENT, ESTABLISHED, etc.). Advantages: more secure than packet filtering. Can detect port scanning, SYN flooding, and spoofed packets that don't match any connection. Disadvantages: higher resource usage. Still cannot inspect application-layer content. Examples: most modern enterprise firewalls include stateful inspection as baseline.

n-layer content. Examples: most modern enterprise firewalls include stateful inspection as baseline. Application Layer Firewall (Proxy Firewall) Operation: acts as a proxy — fully terminates client connections, inspects application-layer content, then initiates new connection to server. Deep Packet Inspection (DPI). Features: understands application protocols (HTTP, FTP, DNS), can block specific URLs, file types, commands, or content. Advantages: most comprehensive inspection.

an block specific URLs, file types, commands, or content. Advantages: most comprehensive inspection. Can prevent application-layer attacks (SQL injection, XSS in HTTP traffic). Disadvantages: slowest, highest resource usage, requires separate proxy for each protocol. Examples: Squid (HTTP proxy), Snort with application rules, Palo Alto App-ID.

oxy for each protocol. Examples: Squid (HTTP proxy), Snort with application rules, Palo Alto App-ID. Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) Features: combines stateful inspection + deep packet inspection + application awareness + user identity + IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) + SSL inspection + URL filtering + threat intelligence. Application Control: can identify and control specific applications regardless of port (e.g., block Facebook but allow Office 365).

fy and control specific applications regardless of port (e.g., block Facebook but allow Office 365). SSL/TLS Inspection: decrypts, inspects, and re-encrypts HTTPS traffic — essential since most malware uses HTTPS. Examples: Palo Alto PA-series, Fortinet FortiGate, Cisco Firepower, Check Point. 8.3.2 Firewall Architectures Screening Router: single router with packet-filtering rules. Simple, but weak. Dual-Homed Host: computer with two NICs — one connected to internet, one to internal network.

weak. Dual-Homed Host: computer with two NICs — one connected to internet, one to internal network. Acts as firewall and proxy. Routing disabled to force all traffic through proxy. Screened Host Architecture: combines packet-filtering router with a bastion host (proxy/gateway). Router filters most traffic; bastion host handles allowed traffic.

h a bastion host (proxy/gateway). Router filters most traffic; bastion host handles allowed traffic. Screened Subnet (DMZ Architecture): two firewalls create a DMZ (De-Militarized Zone) — a semi-trusted network for publicly accessible servers (web, mail, DNS). Internal network is double-protected. 8.3.3 DMZ (De-Militarized Zone) A DMZ is a separate network segment between the internet and the internal trusted network.

zed Zone) A DMZ is a separate network segment between the internet and the internal trusted network. Public-facing servers (web servers, mail servers, DNS) are placed in the DMZ — accessible from internet but isolated from internal network. Even if a DMZ server is compromised, the attacker cannot directly access internal systems. 8.4 IP Security (IPSec) IPSec is a suite of protocols that provides security at the IP layer (Layer 3).

IP Security (IPSec) IPSec is a suite of protocols that provides security at the IP layer (Layer 3). It can encrypt and authenticate all IP traffic regardless of the application. Operates in two modes. 8.4.1 IPSec Protocols AH (Authentication Header): provides authentication and integrity for IP packets (including parts of the IP header). Does NOT provide encryption — confidentiality. Uses HMAC. Protocol number 51.

rts of the IP header). Does NOT provide encryption — confidentiality. Uses HMAC. Protocol number 51. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload): provides encryption, authentication, and integrity for IP payload. Most commonly used. Protocol number 50. IKE (Internet Key Exchange): protocol for negotiating and establishing IPSec security associations (SAs) and exchanging encryption keys. IKEv2 is current version.

blishing IPSec security associations (SAs) and exchanging encryption keys. IKEv2 is current version. SA (Security Association): one-directional 'agreement' on security parameters between two IPSec peers. Defined by SPI (Security Parameter Index), destination IP, and protocol (AH or ESP). IPSec connections require at least two SAs (one in each direction). SAD and SPD: Security Association Database (stores active SAs) and Security Policy Database (defines which traffic requires IPSec treatment).

e (stores active SAs) and Security Policy Database (defines which traffic requires IPSec treatment). 8.4.2 IPSec Modes Transport Mode: only the IP payload (TCP/UDP data) is encrypted/authenticated. Original IP header preserved. Used for host-to-host communication (e.g., securing management traffic between two servers). Tunnel Mode: the entire original IP packet (header + payload) is encrypted and encapsulated in a new IP packet with a new outer IP header.

cket (header + payload) is encrypted and encapsulated in a new IP packet with a new outer IP header. Used for VPNs — gateways add outer IP headers for routing. 8.5 Virtual Private Networks (VPN) A VPN creates a secure, encrypted 'tunnel' over a public network (typically the Internet), allowing remote users or sites to communicate as if they were directly connected on a private network. 8.5.

g remote users or sites to communicate as if they were directly connected on a private network. 8.5.1 Types of VPNs Remote Access VPN (Client-to-Site): individual users connect to the corporate network from remote locations. User installs VPN client (Cisco AnyConnect, FortiClient, OpenVPN client). SSL VPN or IPSec VPN. Site-to-Site VPN (Gateway-to-Gateway): connects entire networks at different locations (headquarters ↔ branch office). Uses dedicated VPN gateways at each site.

rks at different locations (headquarters ↔ branch office). Uses dedicated VPN gateways at each site. Typically IPSec-based. SSL VPN: uses TLS (port 443) — works through most firewalls and proxies. Browser-based or thin-client. Increasingly popular for remote access. IPSec VPN: uses IPSec protocols in tunnel mode. Very secure, full network-level encryption. Common for site-to-site. PPTP (Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol): old, fast but weak encryption (MS-CHAPv2).

r site-to-site. PPTP (Point-to-Point Tunneling Protocol): old, fast but weak encryption (MS-CHAPv2). Largely deprecated due to known vulnerabilities. L2TP/IPSec: L2TP tunneling + IPSec encryption. More secure than PPTP but slower. OpenVPN: open-source SSL VPN. Highly configurable, strong security, cross-platform. Uses TLS for key exchange and OpenSSL for encryption. WireGuard: modern, minimal, high-performance VPN protocol. Uses state-of-the-art cryptography (ChaCha20, Curve25519, BLAKE2).

l, high-performance VPN protocol. Uses state-of-the-art cryptography (ChaCha20, Curve25519, BLAKE2). Much simpler codebase than IPSec (~4,000 lines vs ~400,000). 8.5.2 VPN Security Mechanisms Tunneling: encapsulating one protocol inside another — the private packet travels inside a public-network packet. Encryption: all data inside the tunnel is encrypted — confidentiality even over public internet.

et. Encryption: all data inside the tunnel is encrypted — confidentiality even over public internet. Authentication: both endpoints authenticate before tunnel is established — using certificates, pre-shared keys, or user credentials. Integrity: HMAC or AEAD ensures data cannot be modified in transit. Split Tunneling: VPN only routes specific traffic (e.g., corporate traffic) through the tunnel; internet traffic goes directly. Reduces VPN load but may expose corporate devices.

rough the tunnel; internet traffic goes directly. Reduces VPN load but may expose corporate devices. Kill Switch: blocks all internet traffic if VPN connection drops — prevents accidental unencrypted data leakage. 8.6 Intrusion Detection and Prevention (IDS/IPS) IDS (Intrusion Detection System): monitors network/system traffic for suspicious activity and generates alerts. Passive — detects but does not block. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System): actively blocks detected intrusions in real-time.

does not block. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System): actively blocks detected intrusions in real-time. Inline deployment — sits in the traffic path. NIDS (Network IDS): monitors network traffic at strategic points. E.g., Snort, Suricata. HIDS (Host IDS): monitors host system events — file system changes, log files, process activity. E.g., OSSEC, Tripwire. Signature-Based Detection: matches traffic against known attack patterns (signatures). Fast, accurate for known attacks.

ction: matches traffic against known attack patterns (signatures). Fast, accurate for known attacks. Cannot detect zero-days. Anomaly-Based Detection: establishes baseline of normal behaviour; alerts on deviations. Can detect novel attacks but high false-positive rate. Stateful Protocol Analysis: compares observed traffic to expected protocol behaviour. 8.7 Intrusion (Attacks and Response) 8.7.

compares observed traffic to expected protocol behaviour. 8.7 Intrusion (Attacks and Response) 8.7.1 Phases of a Network Intrusion Reconnaissance: attacker gathers information — OSINT, port scanning, banner grabbing, network mapping. Passive (no direct contact) or active (direct probing). Scanning and Enumeration: identifying open ports, services, operating systems, user accounts, and vulnerabilities. Tools: Nmap, Nessus, Nikto.

ports, services, operating systems, user accounts, and vulnerabilities. Tools: Nmap, Nessus, Nikto. Gaining Access: exploiting a vulnerability — unpatched software, weak credentials, misconfigurations, social engineering. Maintaining Access (Persistence): installing backdoors, rootkits, creating rogue admin accounts — ensures continued access even if initial vulnerability is patched.

, creating rogue admin accounts — ensures continued access even if initial vulnerability is patched. Lateral Movement: moving from the initial compromised system to other systems in the network — pivoting, credential theft. Data Exfiltration: stealing sensitive data — often over encrypted channels to evade detection. Covering Tracks: deleting logs, clearing event history to hide evidence of intrusion. 8.7.

etection. Covering Tracks: deleting logs, clearing event history to hide evidence of intrusion. 8.7.2 Incident Response Process Preparation: establish IR team, policies, tools, and communication plans before an incident. Identification: detect and confirm the incident — distinguish real attacks from false positives. Containment: limit the spread — isolate compromised systems, block attacker's network access. Eradication: remove malware, close vulnerabilities, eliminate attacker's access.

r's network access. Eradication: remove malware, close vulnerabilities, eliminate attacker's access. Recovery: restore systems from clean backups, validate system integrity, resume operations. Lessons Learned (Post-Incident): analyse what happened, improve defences, update policies and procedures.

Topic Introduction and Discussion During the internship, I worked on three independent but thematically related projects, all centred on applying AI / data-driven techniques to practical enterprise problems: information retrieval and question-answering over documents, predictive inventory management for industrial spare parts, and AI-assisted image processing for digital content.

ventory management for industrial spare parts, and AI-assisted image processing for digital content. Stack 5.1 AI Knowledge Assistant Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) / Document Q&A FastAPI, Next.js, ChromaDB, BM25, Groq LLM 5.2 ImageAI - Smart-Crop, Compare & Regenerate API Computer Vision / Image Processing .NET 10, ONNX Runtime (DETR), SixLabors ImageSharp 5.3 Predictive Inventory Forecasting & Planning Dashboard Time-Series Forecasting / Inventory Analytics Streamlit, Prophet, Azure SQL Server, Plotly 19 5.1 AI Knowledge Assistant (RAG-Based Document Q&A System) 5.1.

Prophet, Azure SQL Server, Plotly 19 5.1 AI Knowledge Assistant (RAG-Based Document Q&A System) 5.1.1 Problem Statement Employees within an organisation routinely need to search through scattered documents - policies, SOPs, onboarding guides, product notes - to find specific information. Manual searching is slow, and naive keyword search often misses relevant content that is phrased differently from the query.

, and naive keyword search often misses relevant content that is phrased differently from the query. At the same time, organisations are wary of adopting LLM-based assistants because of the risk of hallucinated answers and the exposure of sensitive personal information (PII). The AI Knowledge Assistant addresses this by providing a chat-based interface where users can upload their own documents and ask natural-language questions.

chat-based interface where users can upload their own documents and ask natural-language questions. The system must retrieve the most relevant passages from the uploaded documents, generate a grounded answer with citations to the source documents, redact any PII before returning the response, and provide an automated evaluation framework to measure answer quality. 5.1.

eturning the response, and provide an automated evaluation framework to measure answer quality. 5.1.2 System Architecture The application follows a client-server architecture with a hybrid two-stage retrieval pipeline and a two-pass generation pipeline: User (Browser) | v Next.

The AI Knowledge Assistant therefore combines both, followed by a re-ranking step:  Stage 1A - Bi-Encoder (Semantic Search): The user's query is embedded using all- MiniLM-L6-v2 and matched against document-chunk embeddings stored in ChromaDB to retrieve the top semantically similar chunks.

gainst document-chunk embeddings stored in ChromaDB to retrieve the top semantically similar chunks.  Stage 1B - BM25 (Keyword Search): The same query is scored against a BM25 keyword index (rank-bm25) built over the same chunks, capturing exact term / lexical matches.  Stage 1C - Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): The two ranked lists from Stages 1A and 1B are merged into a single candidate list using RRF, which favours chunks that rank highly in either (or both) lists.

a single candidate list using RRF, which favours chunks that rank highly in either (or both) lists.  Stage 2 - Cross-Encoder Re-ranking: The fused candidates are re-scored with the cross- encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 model, which jointly encodes the query and each candidate chunk for a much finer-grained relevance score, and the top-K chunks are passed to the generation stage. Two-Pass Generation, Verification and PII Redaction  Pass 1 (Build & Generate): The prompt_builder.py module assembles a prompt from the re-ranked chunks and conversation history; this prompt is sent to Groq's llama-3.3-70b- versatile model, which generates a draft answer with inline citations to the source documents.

-70b- versatile model, which generates a draft answer with inline citations to the source documents.  Pass 2 (Verify / Anti-Hallucination): The draft answer is checked against the retrieved context in a second LLM pass to catch and remove claims that are not supported by the retrieved chunks - if the model cannot find an answer in the documents, it is instructed to honestly respond “I don't know” rather than guess.  PII Redaction: Before the final response is returned to the user, redactor.

ow” rather than guess.  PII Redaction: Before the final response is returned to the user, redactor.py applies a set of regular expressions to mask personally identifiable information (e.g., emails, phone numbers) that may appear in the retrieved context or generated answer. ounding (the proportion of the answer that is verifiably supported by the retrieved 28 context), and Average Latency (end-to-end response time). he DOCX/Excel export endpoints, and ran the automated evaluation suite to produce the evaluation report shown above. 33 5.2 ImageAI - Smart-Crop, Compare and Regenerate API 5.2.1 Problem Statement The 366Pi Technologies product team had an existing image-processing pipeline that relied on a remotely-hosted HuggingFace Stable-Diffusion XL Refiner API to regenerate and enhance sports and portrait photographs.

ggingFace Stable-Diffusion XL Refiner API to regenerate and enhance sports and portrait photographs. While the output quality was acceptable, every single request incurred a 20- to 90-second round-trip latency — a consequence of cold-start delays, model-loading overhead, and network transfer of full-resolution images to and from an external inference server. This response time made the pipeline impractical for any near-real-time editorial or production workflow.

response time made the pipeline impractical for any near-real-time editorial or production workflow. Beyond latency, the hosted-model approach introduced three additional risks: (i) dependency on a third-party API subject to rate-limits and service outages, (ii) potential copyright and privacy concerns from uploading client images to an external service, and (iii) no mechanism to quantify how similar a regenerated image was to the original — a critical metric for determining copyright safety bef

lar a regenerated image was to the original — a critical metric for determining copyright safety before publishing. The team therefore required a fully local, self-contained pipeline that could perform smart cropping, style-preserving regeneration, and similarity scoring without any external API calls. The ImageAI project was scoped to deliver exactly this: an ASP.NET Core (.

t any external API calls. The ImageAI project was scoped to deliver exactly this: an ASP.NET Core (.NET 10) REST API hosting three specialised services — SmartCropService (DETR-based subject detection and Rule-of-Thirds composition), ImageRegenerateService (local two-pass colour-grading with seven style presets), and ImageCompareService (four-metric similarity and copyright-safety scoring) — all running entirely on- premises using ONNX Runtime and SixLabors.

and copyright-safety scoring) — all running entirely on- premises using ONNX Runtime and SixLabors.ImageSharp, with sub-second response times replacing the previous 20-90 second API calls. 5.2.2 Project Evolution: From a Hosted Diffusion Model to a Local ONNX Pipeline As part of the company's research initiative, there was a need for a backend service that could take a raw photo (e.g.

pany's research initiative, there was a need for a backend service that could take a raw photo (e.g., a sports image) and produce edited variants suitable for digital- marketing/social-media use: a subject-aware “smart crop” to a target aspect ratio, a colour- graded “regenerated” version with a chosen visual style, and a way to quantitatively compare the edited output against the original - both for quality control and to assess copyright “transformativeness”.

output against the original - both for quality control and to assess copyright “transformativeness”. The service was therefore re-architected to run entirely locally: object/subject detection now uses a local ONNX export of facebook/detr-resnet-50 (DETR) running on ONNX Runtime 1.26.0, and colour grading / regeneration is performed with deterministic image-processing operations using SixLabors.ImageSharp 3.1.8 (Apache 2.0 licensed - chosen over 34 ImageSharp v4.

ing operations using SixLabors.ImageSharp 3.1.8 (Apache 2.0 licensed - chosen over 34 ImageSharp v4.0+, which requires a paid commercial licence). This local pipeline is instant, free, unlimited, and produces realistic, reproducible output.  The free tier was rate-limited to roughly 1,000 requests per day, and behaviour was otherwise unpredictable.  Cold-start latency of 20-90 seconds per request made the API impractical for interactive use.

ble.  Cold-start latency of 20-90 seconds per request made the API impractical for interactive use.  Output images looked “painted” / artistic rather than photo-realistic, which was unsuitable for sports/marketing imagery. The first version of this service used the Hugging Face Inference API to call a Stable Diffusion XL Refiner model for image regeneration. SmartCropService — performs subject-aware cropping using the locally hosted DETR ONNX model.

points. SmartCropService — performs subject-aware cropping using the locally hosted DETR ONNX model. Detected bounding boxes are passed to a rule-of-thirds composition algorithm that positions the subject at a visually balanced anchor point within the requested output dimensions. ImageRegenerateService — applies a two-pass colour-grading pipeline. In the first pass the uploaded image is analysed to infer the sport context (hockey, basketball, or generic).

rst pass the uploaded image is analysed to infer the sport context (hockey, basketball, or generic). imilarity and copyright-safety metrics between the original uploaded image and the processed output. fety scoring formula in ImageCompareService.cs, and wired up all three endpoints in ImageController.cs, validating the complete API via Swagger UI across real sports and portrait images. 51 5.3 Predictive Inventory Forecasting and Planning Dashboard 5.3.1 Problem Statement A Power & Energy sector client address a critical operational gap: the procurement and maintenance teams had no automated mechanism to forecast spare-parts demand.

ap: the procurement and maintenance teams had no automated mechanism to forecast spare-parts demand. The stock register spanned over 115,000 transaction records across seven years of IN and OUT stock movements, but existed only as a flat database table with no analytical layer on top.

and OUT stock movements, but existed only as a flat database table with no analytical layer on top. The project objective was to design and deploy an end-to-end Python pipeline that would ingest the raw Stock_Register data, apply per-item Prophet time-series forecasting, classify each item's replenishment risk as CRITICAL / HIGH / WARNING / NORMAL, compute safety-stock and reorder quantities, and surface all results through an interactive Streamlit dashboard with automated Excel and DOCX report
surface all results through an interactive Streamlit dashboard with automated Excel and DOCX report export. 5.3.

results through an interactive Streamlit dashboard with automated Excel and DOCX report export. 5.3.2 System Pipeline The Inventory Forecasting system was built to forecast future demand for each spare part using historical consumption data, combine this with planned PM demand, compute the recommended reorder quantity against current stock levels, classify each item into a risk category, and present all of this through an interactive Streamlit dashboard - including scenario (“what-if”) analysis

nt all of this through an interactive Streamlit dashboard - including scenario (“what-if”) analysis and a historical stock-movement / replenishment-health analysis. A Power & Energy (PE) sector client operates a large fleet of diesel-generator (DG) sets and must maintain an inventory of spare parts (gaskets, relays, regulators, alternators, etc.) to support both normal demand and scheduled Preventive Maintenance (PM) activities.

ternators, etc.) to support both normal demand and scheduled Preventive Maintenance (PM) activities. Manually estimating how much of each part to keep in stock is error-prone: too little stock risks a CRITICAL shortage that can halt operations, while too much stock ties up working capital. historical demand records from the forecast_ready_dataset table in Azure SQL Server. 2 forecasting.py Runs Facebook Prophet time-series forecasting for each item over the selected forecast horizon. 3 pm_engine.py Adds planned Preventive-Maintenance demand for each item from the pm_demand_output table. 4 inventory_engine.py Combines forecast demand + PM demand, and 52 computes safety stock and recommended_reorder_qty against available_stock from Stock_Register. 5 alert_engine.

afety stock and recommended_reorder_qty against available_stock from Stock_Register. 5 alert_engine.py Classifies each item into CRITICAL / HIGH / WARNING / NORMAL risk based on the projected shortfall and stock-coverage days. - main.py (orchestrator) run_pipeline(horizon, scenario, max_items) ties together Steps 1-5, writes logs and the procurement output. - stock_movement.

It reads from four key tables - forecast_ready_dataset (historical demand), pm_demand_output (planned PM demand), Stock_Register (current stock levels and 7-year transaction history) and forecast_output (pipeline results). 5.3.6 Forecast Accuracy Metrics The pipeline evaluates forecast accuracy for each item using a rolling train/test split.

acy Metrics The pipeline evaluates forecast accuracy for each item using a rolling train/test split. The last 20% of available historical data (minimum five rows) is withheld as a test set. A Prophet model is trained on 54 the remaining rows, predictions are generated for the held-out period, and three error metrics are computed per item: Mean Absolute Error (MAE): average absolute deviation between predicted and actual demand values.

m: Mean Absolute Error (MAE): average absolute deviation between predicted and actual demand values. Lower MAE indicates that the model's central estimate is close to reality on a typical day. Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): penalises larger errors more heavily than MAE, making it sensitive to occasional demand spikes — useful for items with volatile consumption patterns.

king it sensitive to occasional demand spikes — useful for items with volatile consumption patterns. Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): expresses error as a percentage of actual demand, enabling direct cross-item comparison regardless of quantity scale. Items with fewer than 15 data points are excluded from accuracy scoring and flagged as insufficient.

Items with fewer than 15 data points are excluded from accuracy scoring and flagged as insufficient. Findings 7.1 AI Knowledge Assistant  The hybrid retrieval pipeline (bi-encoder + BM25 + RRF fusion + cross-encoder re- ranking) consistently surfaced the correct source document for both factual and paraphrased queries during testing - e.g., the “onboarding process” query in Figure 2 correctly cited both onboarding_sop.json and ECommerce_Notes.docx.

arding process” query in Figure 2 correctly cited both onboarding_sop.json and ECommerce_Notes.docx.  The two-pass generation/verification flow combined with PII redaction provides a practical mechanism to reduce hallucination risk and protect sensitive data, without requiring a fine-tuned model.  The automated evaluation report (Figure 5) gives a quick, repeatable way to monitor Recall@K, Citation Coverage, Answer Grounding and Latency after any change to the retrieval or prompting logic. 7.

tion Coverage, Answer Grounding and Latency after any change to the retrieval or prompting logic. 7.2 ImageAI - Smart-Crop, Compare and Regenerate API Figure 22: Smart-crop output for the Lamine image showing a reproducible “ghosting” / double-exposure artefact around the subject (overallChangePercent = 48.1%, identical across two independent runs).

e artefact around the subject (overallChangePercent = 48.1%, identical across two independent runs).  A reproducible artefact was found in the SmartCrop service: when smart-cropping the Lamine image, the output exhibits a visible “ghosting” / double-exposure effect around the subject (Figure 17). Re-running the identical request produced byte-for-byte identical similarity metrics (48.1% / 48.1% / 9.1% / 17.

he identical request produced byte-for-byte identical similarity metrics (48.1% / 48.1% / 9.1% / 17.3 / Moderate, tests #2 and #4 in Table 14), proving the artefact is deterministic and reproducible rather than a one-off glitch. This points to a likely root cause of overlapping / duplicate bounding boxes returned by the 71 DETR detection step being blended together during cropping, and is recorded here as a known issue for future investigation (see Section 9).

her during cropping, and is recorded here as a known issue for future investigation (see Section 9).  The copyright-safety scoring formula behaved sensibly across all six test calls: large structural edits (e.g., the hockey image's 99.1% overall change from smart-cropping) scored High, while a moderate-change smart-crop of the Lamine image (48.1% overall change) scored Moderate.

High, while a moderate-change smart-crop of the Lamine image (48.1% overall change) scored Moderate.  Replacing the hosted Stable Diffusion XL Refiner API with a local ONNX DETR + ImageSharp pipeline eliminated the 20-90 second cold-start latency and the ~1,000 request/day rate limit, while producing more photo-realistic output suitable for sports/marketing imagery. 7.

Conclusion The six-week internship at 366Pi Technologies, Ranchi, provided substantial hands-on exposure to building production-style AI and data-driven applications across three very different domains - document-based question answering, time-series demand forecasting, and computer-vision-based image editing.

t-based question answering, time-series demand forecasting, and computer-vision-based image editing. On the AI Knowledge Assistant, I learned how to design and implement a hybrid retrieval pipeline combining semantic search, keyword search, fusion and re-ranking, and how to use a two-pass LLM generation strategy together with PII redaction to build a grounded, trustworthy chat assistant.

LLM generation strategy together with PII redaction to build a grounded, trustworthy chat assistant. On the Inventory Forecasting project, I gained practical experience with Facebook Prophet, SQL-Server-backed data pipelines, and translating raw forecasts into actionable business outputs - safety stock, reorder quantities, risk classifications and scenario analysis - through an interactive Streamlit dashboard.

quantities, risk classifications and scenario analysis - through an interactive Streamlit dashboard. On the ImageAI project, I worked with ONNX Runtime and a local DETR model inside an ASP.NET Core service, implemented image-processing and colour-grading logic, and developed a quantitative framework for measuring image similarity and copyright “transformativeness”.

eveloped a quantitative framework for measuring image similarity and copyright “transformativeness”. Beyond the specific technologies, the internship strengthened my understanding of the full software-development lifecycle - from reading and extending an existing codebase, to writing tests and evaluation scripts, to documenting and presenting the final outcomes - and gave me a clear view of how individual engineering tasks connect to the broader business goals of an organisation such as 366Pi Te

dividual engineering tasks connect to the broader business goals of an organisation such as 366Pi Technologies. I am confident that the skills and experience gained during this period will be of significant value in my future academic and professional endeavours. 73 9. MedGemma Prescription Review Scenario AI-powered prescription review system Problem Statement Healthcare professionals often work with prescriptions that are handwritten, scanned, or stored as unstructured text, making them difficult to process and review efficiently. Manual extraction of key prescription details such as patient information, medication names, dosage, frequency, duration, and special instructions is time-consuming and prone to human error.

s, dosage, frequency, duration, and special instructions is time-consuming and prone to human error. The challenge is to develop a local-first AI-powered prescription review system that evaluates the capability of MedGemma in understanding prescription images and text.

view system that evaluates the capability of MedGemma in understanding prescription images and text. The system should automatically extract structured prescription information, present the extracted data for human verification, allow reviewers to approve, edit, or reject the results, and store reviewed outputs for future analysis. Solution approach 1. Prescription Upload Allow users to upload prescription images or text files. This makes unstructured prescription data available for processing.

escription images or text files. This makes unstructured prescription data available for processing. 2. Image Preprocessing Improve image quality using techniques like noise removal and contrast enhancement. This helps the AI read prescriptions more accurately. 3. OCR Extraction (Optional) Convert prescription images into text using OCR tools. This makes handwritten or scanned prescriptions easier to analyze. 4.

ges into text using OCR tools. This makes handwritten or scanned prescriptions easier to analyze. 4. MedGemma Analysis Use MedGemma to understand the prescription and extract important details such as patient name, medicines, dosage, and frequency. 5. Structured Data Extraction Convert the AI output into a predefined JSON format. This ensures the extracted information is organized and easy to use. 6.

to a predefined JSON format. This ensures the extracted information is organized and easy to use. 6. Human Review Display the extracted data to a reviewer who can approve, edit, or reject the results. This improves accuracy and reliability. 7. Data Storage Store reviewed prescriptions and extraction results in a local database such as SQLite for future reference. 8. Export Results Allow users to export validated prescription records in JSON or CSV format for reporting and analysis. 9.

users to export validated prescription records in JSON or CSV format for reporting and analysis. 9. Metadata Logging Record model details, latency, prompt templates, and runtime information to evaluate MedGemma's performance.
